### Post by BatPenguin on 2008-11-24
> **amyg said:**
> 
```
09:25:30 URL:http://launcher.turbine.com/serverinfo.php?s=206.16.158.36 [972/972] -> ".launcher/server.config" [1]
sh: Can't open urlencode.sh
sh: Can't open urlencode.sh


```

So you're right, the error has changed.


Judging from the fact that the GUI launcher seems to work and the recent posts between ajackson and you, it seems like you and Ajackson found the problem and it's not the script or the GUI launcher but winetricks and strange home folder. I would not have known that setting something else than /home/username as a home folder causes problems, that was news to me.

But anyway, just for the record about this launcher error: this error message is due to not installing the "urlencode.sh" script, like it says. SNy's lotrolauncher script page describes this and has a link to it, you have to get the script and put it in the lotro folder. It needs to go into the .wine/.../The Lord of the Rings Online directory and be made executable (chmod +x). Anyway, this is clearly not your problem since the GUI seems to work, but that was at least one of the reasons why the script wasn't working. The permissions issue with the launcherscript could still just be that whenever you run something as sudo, the /home/mom/.LotROLinux folder files get owned by root so you have to make sure everything is owned by you before running the script. Whenever you run something as sudo, you might undo the previous permissions changes if config files get modified, so try to resist the temptation :)

Also, I clearly wrote the .winetrickscache folder name wrong: yes, it's "mom" for you, not amy. But I don't know if that will make a difference to change its permissions. Try changing the permissions of the .winetrickscache folder under "mom" (sudo chown amy /home/mom/.winetrickscache -R) and re-running the winetricks script again (not sudo), but I don't think it'll help.

Anyway, if playing before your friend is available to help is important to you, you could do this (should be safe enough):

- make a new user account (Settings->Administration->Users and groups), let the system just make a home folder for it in the normal /home/user manner
- log in as this new user, see if you can download and install winetricks just fine as this user. If not, no need to continue further.
- if winetricks installs, install Lotro from the DVD (do the whole original install including all registry etc. tricks, just like in the beginning, you need to redo these for your new user), but don't bother to patch
- Copy or move (doesn't look like you have disk space to just copy) your Lotro directories from the amy account's .wine directory to the new account. You need to do this as sudo so copy would be safer.
- change all permissions of these directories to those of the new user with chown
- start the launcher as the new user and patch

That SHOULD work. All of the commands involved in these steps (moving/copying, changing permissions) have been covered in earlier posts here, but if you're unsure about commands, go ahead and ask.

By doing this, you'd at least have another account that you can use for Lotro. If you can somehow free up more disk space you wouldn't have to move (delete) the Lotro directories from the amy account, you could just copy them so they'd stay under the amy account too. If you don't have a lot of special customizations etc, you could just start using the new account as your primary one and copy everything else under it too. Might be easier if changing your account won't mess up too many other things.

### Post by SNy on 2008-11-24
> **BatPenguin said:**
> Judging from the fact that the GUI launcher seems to work and the recent posts between ajackson and you, it seems like you and Ajackson found the problem and it's not the script or the GUI launcher but winetricks and strange home folder. I would not have known that setting something else than /home/username as a home folder causes problems, that was news to me.

It shouldn't be a problem normally when $HOME is set properly, unless of course the application in question is poorly written.

> **BatPenguin said:**
> But anyway, just for the record about this launcher error: this error message is due to not installing the "urlencode.sh" script, like it says. SNy's lotrolauncher script page describes this and has a link to it, you have to get the script and put it in the lotro folder. It needs to go into the .wine/.../The Lord of the Rings Online directory and be made executable (chmod +x).

Aye. Also, the same (chmod +x) applies to the launcher script itself. If you don't make it executable, you, well, can't execute it like it was a program. The error you got there tells you so:
> **amyg said:**
> When I try to use it without sudo :
```
amy@mom-desktop:~/.wine/drive_c/Program Files/Turbine/The Lord of the Rings Online$ ./lotrolauncher.script
bash: ./lotrolauncher.script: Permission denied

```

You can also call it with
```

bash lotrolauncher.script

```

instead, that way you don't need to set the executable bit.

Regarding the script execution results...
> **amyg said:**
> 
Connecting to world login queue for realm Meneldor...
[https://launcher.turbine.com/login_queue.php:](https://launcher.turbine.com/login_queue.php:)
09:25:31 ERROR 404: Not Found.

Error: World login queue response indicates failure.


That is quite strange. Either the permission problem prevented the download of the current setup files from turbine and the queue URL is outdated or there is some problem with the server. Or, third option, the missing urlencode.sh results in a corrupted wget request and that leads to the error.

Anyway, no offense, amyg, I know it tends to be a bit tedious, but do yourself a favour and check the various messages the script, wget & co. print out. It always helps to copy them here verbatim instead of stating that it doesn't work for some obscure reason.

HTH,
SNy

---

With MoM, they're back, at least for the server I am playing on (EU, [DE] Morthond). Within the first couple of days, they seem to be quite populated and time out from time to time.
I haven't gotten around to implement the retry functionality, so if you keep getting dropped out when trying to login with the script, drop me a note.

### Post by ajackson on 2008-11-24
> **amyg said:**
> 
```
err:module:import_dll Library MFC80U.DLL (which is needed by L"C:\\Program Files\\Maxtor\\Sync\\DRVIFNT.dll") not found
err:module:import_dll Library MSVCR80.dll (which is needed by L"C:\\Program Files\\Maxtor\\Sync\\DRVIFNT.dll") not found
err:module:import_dll Library DRVIFNT.dll (which is needed by L"C:\\Program Files\\Maxtor\\Sync\\SyncServices.exe") not found
err:module:import_dll Library MFC80U.DLL (which is needed by L"C:\\Program Files\\Maxtor\\Sync\\SyncServices.exe") not found
err:module:import_dll Library MSVCR80.dll (which is needed by L"C:\\Program Files\\Maxtor\\Sync\\SyncServices.exe") not found
err:module:LdrInitializeThunk Main exe initialization for L"C:\\Program Files\\Maxtor\\Sync\\SyncServices.exe" failed, status c0000135
err:module:import_dll Library MSVCR80.dll (which is needed by L"C:\\Program Files\\Turbine\\The Lord of the Rings Online\\lotroclient.exe") not found
err:module:LdrInitializeThunk Main exe initialization for L"C:\\Program Files\\Turbine\\The Lord of the Rings Online\\lotroclient.exe" failed, status c0000135
```

Having had a re-read of the problem I think your setup is OK (bit unusual but OK) the only real problem is that next to last line, the one about MSVCR80.dll, the ones above it aren't to do with LOTRO but seem to be something to do with maxtor disk drives (some service that seems to be trying to run).

The solution is getting the VC run-time libraries installing correctly.

The 404 error you were getting for the realm could have been because the realm was down rather than anything bad in configuration.

So retry the winetricks
```
bash ./winetricks
```
from the directory containing winetricks.

You might have to remove the .winetrickscache directory to be safe (ie ensure there are no files in there owned by root, so you might have to use sudo rm -rf .winetricks).

The GUI uses $HOME, wine defaults to $HOME/.wine so they won't really be affected by $HOME being slightly non-standard.

### Post by SNy on 2008-11-25
Hm, if I understand that correctly, you did a completely fresh install of LOTRO, including patching via the standalone updates or patcher? You did not copy some stuff over from the old installation?

The reason I am asking is that the error could indicate a problem with the data files being unreadable/writable by you (belonging to root, for instance). You should definitely try to find out more details. I thought that there was also a patch log file, maybe containing a hint.

Regarding the start of the game itself, does it even come up or does it boot you directly back to desktop? If the data files were not up to date due to access restrictions, it should at least get to the point of displaying a black screen 'connection to server has been lost', indicating invalid client files.

---

### Post by ajackson on 2008-11-25
Do you have a UserPreferences.ini in a directory under /home/mom? If so edit that (should just be able to double click it) and look for the AllowFakeFullScreen option and try setting it to False if it is True (should no longer be an issue but in the past it caused the brief flash of a game window follow by it stopping).

---

### Post by ajackson on 2008-11-25
To see if it is a permissions problem (or likely to be) post the output of
```
ls -l /home
```

If amy is the owner of /home/mom then things should (hopefully) point to something other than permission problems. If amy isn't the owner of /home/mom then unless the permissions for group (assuming amy & mom are in the same group) or others allow for full read/write/execute access that indicates where the problem may lie and of course re-raises the "why on earth did your friend change the default home directory" question, sometimes trying to be clever for the sake of being clever is the stupidest thing you can do no offence to your friend of course.

### Post by BatPenguin on 2008-11-25
How fast exactly was the patching and did you use any standalone patches or was it reaaly from the original DVD? The Moria patch (no standalones) took me overnight so I would be suspicious if it patched from original to current in less than several hours. Also, the strange failed to check data / access denied sounds like permissions type of problems, so again, I'd make sure the whole .wine directory and lotro dirs are owned by you. And what about disk space? There was only a few gigs left at that one point, are you sure you're not running out? Those patches are BIG.

Edit: to check free space, do "df -ha". Humor me and empty trash etc. or move some stuff to your other disk (documents) if it looks like you don't have a lot free.

Yes, well, that does sound too fast/good to be true to me. I have a 24M ADSL line and a pretty fast computer and patching took me the whole night, so I would be amazed if someone patched from an early book version to the latest very fast. My wife's 2-3 year-old laptop (playing under XP) took 3 hours to just install the patches AFTER first downloading the standalone Moria updates. So it's probably not patching right for you. And disk space, winetricks and permissions seem to be in order, so yes, patching would appear to be the likeliest problem now.

You could try the standalone patches and if that doesn't work, copying over the game directory from your husband's machine, since you probably know that one is updated now and everything else seems to be ok. Copying from a Windows machine is how we used to have to do it before, so there shouldn't be any problems doing it. Just make sure you fix the permissions after copying, if you can't do it as your normal user account for some reason. I know these suggestions seem to be going around in circles, but it just seems so odd that it's not working for you. It really sounds like everything should be in order.

---

### Post by ajackson on 2008-11-26
> **amyg said:**

Never trust programmers, we really are a bad bunch :) (yes I'm a programmer).

Something does seem to be upsetting the patching process and the starting of wine process but I can't really see what could be doing it.

If you do
```
wine iexplore http://www.google.com
```
Does it show the Google homepage (might ask to install gecko first)? If so that proves that wine is working.

The fact that patching takes 15 mins shows it is doing something, otherwise it would finish in seconds.

It might be best to repost the entire output from running SNy's script, see if there are any strange messages at all from the patcher.

Failing that there is BatPenguin's idea of copying the updated version from a Windows box just to see if it is a patching problem but I would have thought that the client would display an error rather than just quietly die.

### Post by SNy on 2008-11-26
Well, I don't know what would be causing this error amyg postet earlier other than a permission problem on some of the files, maybe the directory, given that the program files seem to get patched just fine.

> Connecting to patch.lotro.com:80
Checking files...files to patch: 0 bytes to download: 0
Patching files:
Connecting to patch.lotro.com:80
checking data...data patches: 0 bytes to download: 0
unlock: 0 empty: 0
result code: 0x80070005
Failed to check data: E_ACCESSDENIED(0x80070005)

Be sure to check all the files and directories for their owner and permissions. Try

```

cd "$HOME/.wine/drive_c/Program Files/Turbine/The Lord of the Rings Online"
find . -not -user amy -ls
```

That will print a list of all files that do not belong to user amy along with their permissions, so you should easily spot any problem.

HTH,
SNy

### Post by CSWScrooge on 2008-11-27
> **johnnydement said:**
> An application has made an attempt to load the C runtime Library incorrectly

Hi, I think this is caused, by the dotnet installation that is automatically done when installing the upgrade from DVD.

The fastest way to fix this is propably to backup your .wine directory and let wine generate a new one.

For standalone patches to work in the future you might want to backup the lotro unsintall registry entries with your original .wine (type regedit and the search for "Turbine" and export the key folder) home directory and import them to the registry of the new wine home directory.

Dont forget to copy lotro to the new .wine directory and to use winetricks (wget [http://www.kegel.com/wine/winetricks](http://www.kegel.com/wine/winetricks)) to reinstall the visual c++ runtime stuff:
winetricks vcrun2005
winetricks vcrun2005sp1

I think that should be all

---

### Post by CSWScrooge on 2008-11-27
> **amyg said:**
> 
result code: 0x80070005
Failed to check data: E_ACCESSDENIED(0x80070005)


I'm not sure if my tale is related to that error, but I've seen it before or at least some similar.

Once upon a time, I had lotro installed on an NTFS partition because I wanted to use the same lotro directory in windows and linux. NTFS was the source for that error. 

However, only one (or maybe two) files were concerned:
in the LotRO game directory the file:
ProjectVersion

If I deleted this file the patcher regenerated it, patched something and with the next call to patchclient.dll the same error occurred.

It looks as if you are using a linux file system (at least if you have not installed ubuntu from windows with wubi). But you could try to delete the mentioned file and restart patching to try to isolate the problem.

I never figured out how make NTFS work, I had to copy the game to a linux filesystem.

Just re-installed my whole machine for reasons beyond this thread. Worked really smooth compared to earlier (0,9,,,) versions of wine. 2 problems are there that were not pre-moria/pre-reinstall:

a) lotro starts and minimizes itself. just like when I change desktops (ctrl+Fx in KDE) and go back, lotro is minimized. No BIG deal, just weird.

b) as soon as I activate post processin effects, doors look all wrong. basically the "outer" door, the one I am supposed to see, gets invisible, turns into a shiny circle of glow, and I see a little tunnel and the "inner" door.

On the bright side, is it just me or do roads render a whole lot better now than they did a while ago? Not as tiled anymore as they used to be...

### Post by BatPenguin on 2008-12-03
> **thom_raindog said:**
> Yeah, no crashing so far. I am not using compiz though, are you? 



As I said, the roads  look really fine now, and I do not even have anisotropic filtering enabled at all :)

Yep, I was using Compiz. It's been working so well lately that I didn't even think of it...but yes, switching workspaces works again after disabling it, thanks for the tip!

Since I really like the effects normally and find them useful, I set up a compizswitcher launcher for myself for Lotro. In case somebody else is interested in using effects normally and disabling them for Lotro, I suggest you do the same. So this just disables compiz effects when you launch the game and re-enables them again when you quit. Very nice little trick, I've been using it with MythTV for a long time. Myth and XBMC don't really like Compiz either. For instructions, see here and substitute LotROLinux for whatever program they have in the examples:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=5307405#post5307405](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=5307405#post5307405)

The only thing I do differently than that page (if I remember correctly what they say) is that I don't rename the original launchers for programs, I put the "compizswitcher" command in a new file (LotROLinux.launch etc.) that then calls the program to start. My reason for this is that if you replace the original file, you have do this all over again whenever you update the program. This way the program can be updated and your launcher will still keep working.

### Post by SNy on 2008-12-04
Ah, that is quite interesting.
Reading Eric Sandalls commments in the wine bugzilla, I experimented a bit myself and found it to be working fine with fluxbox (as Eric mentioned).
Under gnome, I also found it to only affect the game whenever there is a new window w/ decoration and stuff, not by otherwise putting the game out of focus. To elaborate, the window switcher itself does not do harm when you just cycle through the list and return to the game without selecting some other app. But by pressing the PrintScr key for instance the screen capture window gets opened (in the background when the game is stickied) and will cause it to die.
Since I had updated my installation when I got my new hardware, I might have enabled the desktops effects, not sure. Will try the compizswitcher trick later today and report back.

SNy

### Post by BatPenguin on 2008-12-07
> **amyg said:**
> [CODE]
wine: Call from 0x7b844de0 to unimplemented function d3dx9_36.dll.D3DXGetShaderConstantTable, aborting/CODE]


As far as I can tell, this error here is the only one that looks  to be real (maybe). Looks like it could just be a graphics thing now and you might be getting really close. I have no actual idea about what could cause that, so this is just guessing:

- have you installed some native DLLs to help you play another game? You can check this by running "winecfg" from the terminal and then going to the Libraries tab and seeing what it says there, if there's some native stuff listed. If you play other games under wine and have had to follow special directions for using native dlls etc, maybe that could be effecting something. This is just a random guess based on the fact that this file, d3dx9_36.dll, is something I'm pretty sure some games want to have a native version for (Civ4? not sure anymore, I remember messing with it before).

- Do other games sill work? Are you sure your 3D games etc. are working still. You used to play WoW, does that still work fine? And is your resolution in "~/The Lord of the Rings Online/UserPreferences.ini" the right one you've used for the game? If you run run nvidia-settings, what is the graphics driver version?

### Post by BatPenguin on 2008-12-12
> **zami said:**
> 
So, either the patcher wasn't finished, or I should have started with a recent patchclient.dll inserted and patched from there, right?

I'm just copying files from one directory to the other now and crossing my fingers.  :)

### Post by ajackson on 2008-12-12
> **zami said:**
> I'm struggling to understand the update instructions in the lotrolinux how-to.  Do I still need to manually insert the patchclient.dlls, or does the lotrolinux patch take care of that now?
If you installed from a source that wasn't at least book 11 you need to replace the patchclient.dll file as prior to book 11 the patch function isn't exposed in it.

> Also, is there a way to tell when the patcher is finished?  It's been running (yay!!) for just under 2 hours now, and it's chugged through a lot of files.  But I can't tell now if it's finished, or stalled, or what.
As BatPenguin said big patches tend to cause it to hang, I know why but it's nothing I can fix (the patch server doesn't drop the connection so the GUI sits waiting for more patches).

> LotroLinux, lotorlinux-gecko 0.9.2 
Update that to version 0.9.7, it contains a few bug fixes and fixes the problem with how the login process seems to have changed.

from the registry  (I'd had it before but I guess I typo'd somewhere)

Sorry for the non-researched answer (no time to really look up now, gotta run) but a quick "hope this helps" post:

- is that the latest ATI driver? You NEED to be install the ATI driver from their web site. The default Ubuntu FGRLX driver will not work. Really. Won't work. Get the one from ATI's web site and install it. Look up guidelines from either here (see my posts, maybe I posted a link to a how-to) or look up "how to install ati driver" or something like this.
- ATI's driver, ummm, well, how should I pust this....sucks? So disable post-processing for good. It will create black screens (only after the intro screen, though).
- about the userpreferences.ini file, could it be that CXgames saves things into different directories than plain wine? I really don't know. Ajacson should be able to answer this is a heartbeeat, if he sees this, I think he uses it. I actually downloaded crossover games on the free for all day, but never got around to install it since everything I use (Civ4, Lotro) seems to work fine without it. Oh well.

### Post by BatPenguin on 2008-12-14
> **zami said:**
> Do any/many people have a good framerate with LOTRO on Linux, and an ATI card?

I'm really struggling to get past this "moving through soup", unplayably bad framreate, and I'm starting to question if it's something I can control, or an ATI issue.

Ubuntu 8.10, Crossover 7.1.0 (running Wine 1.1.0), 2Gig RAM, Pentium4 3Gig processor, ATI Radeon HD3850 512meg video card (with proprietary ATI drivers)

Well, your machine is certainly in a different league than the 1.8 GHz ATI X700 Mobility Radeon that I've tried to play Lotro with, so I would assume you should be geting a playable framerate, but I just don't know - there hasn't been many (or any) success stories for ATI here. We play under Windows with that laptop now, but last time I tried it (a few months ago), I thought there was some improvement compared to the previous ATI driver. But still, it was laggy with the graphics turned way down (and post-processing off, it doesn't work).

My suggestion is to make sure it's the latest ATI driver from the website (not sure what number but I am sure that what Ubuntu ships is, or used to be, nowhere near new)  and then do the brutal "set a low resolution and turn everything off and work yourself up" method with both the ATI control panel (make sure anti-aliasing etc. are off) and the game settings. Some effects may be total framerate killers, such as shadows. Post-processing makes everything black for me, like I've mentioned. And turning on anti-aliasing in the game settings will turn it into a slide show (with no visible anti-aliasing even seen) on both ATI and Nvidia cards for me. I thought I saw strange framerate effects from certain settings (like "it shouldn't make this much of a difference") so starting with 800x600 and nothing on is a good way to go. Also, at least with the Nvidia driver, you have to restart the game after you play with the anisotrophic filtering settings, to make them go into effect.

The good news is that your ATI card is an actual card, not a laptop component...and decent Nvidia cards are not that expensive anymore. If everything all fails, there's always Santa, ho ho ho :p

[/CODE]

Is this the vcrun libraries (2005 and 2005sp1) ?  They seem to be installed, and I know they were at least earlier.  I don't know, I knew I shouldn't have messed with stuff while I was too sick to think clearly.

You know what, Amyg - you're in luck, I just ran into this same error message :p

This happened after trying to patch up our backroom computer for playing (haven't played there in a while). I had copied over the Lotro directory from the main computer, re-ran the GUI patcher, it seemed to be patched, so I started it - and then this error happened. Or rather, the GUI just closed, nothing happened. With the script launcher I saw it was this error. I fixed it by reinstalling the vncrun libraries with winetricks, then started GUI patching again and there was suddenly more stuff to patch. Apparently without the vcrun stuff properly installed, the patcher will think everything's patched before it is.

So yep, reinstall the vncrun stuff again and then try. Worked for me.

### Post by SNy on 2008-12-17
> **pandayanyan said:**
> Just in case it did work and i couldnt see it or something i decided to check theprocess once more.
 now when i patch (using the terminal script loader) it says 

```

Do you want to check for updates (y/N)? y
Checking for updates...
err:rundll32:WinMain Unable to load L"PatchClient"
err:rundll32:WinMain Unable to load L"PatchClient"
err:rundll32:WinMain Unable to load L"PatchClient"


```


Yeah, the patch process somehow forgets to put the library in place again. Had the same problem as outlined [here](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=6205731&postcount=1575).

Copy over the dll from $GAMEDIR/temp/patchclient.dll to $GAMEDIR/PatchClient.dll and that error is gone.
Then install vcrun and vcrunsp1 with winetricks and you should be good to go.

HTH,
SNy

---

### Post by ajackson on 2008-12-17
I've just tried updating my non-MoM install, apart from having to manually fix the patchclient.dll (as outlined by SNy above), it went fine.

So the people who are getting strange errors on the VCRun install, what version of wine are you using? If it isn't wine 1.1.10, try again with that (in case there is something that has been fixed that caused the problem).

Another thing to try is renaming .wine to something safe (to avoid damaging it) and try using winetricks on a fresh .wine (ie nothing else installed), if that works then it points to some conflict between other things installed in your original wine directory.

If you still keep getting the msvr error (memory has gone can't remember the proper filename :)), try copying that file into your LOTRO directory as that would be the first place wine will look for components.

Oh and try not to create duplicate threads about a problem, all it does is make you look like an attention seeker, help is being given in this thread, multiple threads just cause confusion.

---

### Post by SNy on 2008-12-18
OK, glad you got it working, though I wonder why you installed DX9.

An interesting fact is that, apparently, the patching process for program files differs from that for the data files. While you are not the first one that had a problem with patching the latter, your log indicates that all of those errors might be down to the library dependency on the new VC runtime.

That at least might give other people a hint as to where start looking, so thanks for the heads up.

SNy

Anyway, I think your problem is probably the winetricks/vncrun install. Basically it's as simple as this: You need to download winetricks and use it to install vncrun and vncrunsp1 because of changes done in the Moria update. Then it should patch to the latest version properly. Some people have had problems with this, so it's worth reading through.

### Post by mrkennie on 2008-12-31
I found this happens because the LOTRO installer installs MSVC runtime (just after trying to install .NET) which seems to screw everything up. Not sure, but maybe this is why the download version is needed to install? I fixed this by simply creating a new wine environment and copying the game folder over. Everything worked fine for me after that.

### Post by Bubblesix on 2009-01-01
This is not my first linux experience, but this time it's meant to last, as ubuntu got me interested..
That's why i was trying to adapt my lifestyle to it: not difficult for the most of the applications i generally use, LotRO has been the first obstacle.

I have a dual boot system, XP - Ubuntu 8.10 (intrepid, kernel 2.6.26-8 - not updating to 2.6.28 for the time as my bluetooth wouldn't work with the last kernels), and I decided to leave the previous (updated) lotro installation on the windows partition (and launch it from there).

First of all, I installed wine, version 1.0.1, and followed the lotrolinux.com guide.
I've tried the GUI launcher, but it never worked for me. It was able to read the configuration files, but it never went further than that: "Error accessing GLS data centre.". Took a little peek at the source code, the exact point where it stuck was in
```

GLSDataCentre.cs:
Stream bodyStream = dcRequest.GetRequestStream();
StreamWriter bodyStreamWrite = new StreamWriter(bodyStream);
```

I just tried to work with the 2 scripts suggested in the guide, afterwards:
[LIST]
[*]lotrolauncher.script
[*]urlencode.sh
[/LIST]
placed them in the Lotro main folder, and they worked like a charm, after i learnt the only way to start it was to type (in a terminal window)
```

$ cd /path-to-lotro/
$ ./lotrolauncher.script

```
Instead, invoking:
```

sh lotrolauncher.script

```
was allowing it to connect, but threw some jibberish when it came to show me the server list.

Anyway, i got to the server list, choosed my server, and the launcher script managed to call the client. Wine threw an error about me not having 
```
msvcr80.dll
```
Fine, found this amazing thread, downloaded "winetricks.sh", obtained the "MS Visual C++ 2005 libraries" and the relative service pack.

The game was finally ok and starting. At the first run, it created the  
"UserPreferences.ini" in
```
"windows/profiles/-UserName-/My Documents/The Lord of the Rings Online"
```
The first (and only, up until now) noticeable thing was not a lack of framerate, though. It was a partially unreadable text.

I thought about video card compatibility (i even tried to update directX 9 with winetricks), as wine log some problems right after the start of the game:
```

err:ole:CoGetClassObject class {4590f811-1d3a-11d0-891f-00aa004b2e24} not registered
err:ole:CoGetClassObject no class object {4590f811-1d3a-11d0-891f-00aa004b2e24} could be created for context 0x1
err:ole:CoGetClassObject class {4590f811-1d3a-11d0-891f-00aa004b2e24} not registered
err:ole:CoGetClassObject no class object {4590f811-1d3a-11d0-891f-00aa004b2e24} could be created for context 0x1
err:ole:CoGetClassObject class {4590f811-1d3a-11d0-891f-00aa004b2e24} not registered
err:ole:CoGetClassObject no class object {4590f811-1d3a-11d0-891f-00aa004b2e24} could be created for context 0x1
err:d3d:WineD3D_ChoosePixelFormat Can't find a suitable iPixelFormat

```

That said, here's my system info:
```

$ glxinfo |grep OpenGL
OpenGL vendor string: ATI Technologies Inc.
OpenGL renderer string: ATI MOBILITY RADEON X300
OpenGL version string: 2.1.8201 Release
OpenGL shading language version string: 1.20

### Post by BatPenguin on 2009-01-01
> **Bubblesix said:**
> 
The game was finally ok and starting. At the first run, it created the  
"UserPreferences.ini" in
```
"windows/profiles/-UserName-/My Documents/The Lord of the Rings Online"
```


Well this seems a bit odd, actually. The UserPreferences.ini is created in ~/The Lord of the Rings Online, not under the wine directory structure. Are changes you make to it taking effect in the game?

I have a dual-boot ATI laptop that I occasionally test running Lotro under Ubuntu (it's used for playing it pretty much daily under XP), and since the framerate and other issues (no post-processing) on that machine are pretty bad, I didn't think it's even possible to get to a playable state, really. So I wouldn't be suprised if your text issues are due to the video card. But that's just a guess: my machine (X700 mobility) doesn't have text issues, just terrible frame rate and no post-processing. Have you tried setting the text settings in the game itself? I noticed that quite a few game settings make a big difference, you can actually fix the issues with floating names by selecting "floating names always on top" there in the game. So try changing color and size, if possible / don't remember, in the game. Maybe that'll help.

When I first got it running under Ubuntu I **had to** install the driver from ATI's site, so that's news to me that it works without the latest one. I guess Ibex must come with a recent enough driver. Installing DirectX under wine is not, as far as I know, recommended and certainly not needed for Lotro.

Let us know how it goes and post a screenshot when you can. It'll be good to hear some possible success stories under ATI.

### Post by Bubblesix on 2009-01-02
I used to get a "failed to initialize" when, following the lotrolinux.com guide, I installed .NET 1.1 because of this statement:
> With Wine 1.1.2 you no longer need to use the registry hack as .NET 1.1 (including service pack 1) will install cleanly.

Now.."cleanly" is arguable,as the the core files went in pretty fine, but the service pack refused even to start. Long story short, they were hindering me, blocking a correct execution of the game (with an error on msvcr80.dll).

Had to delete 
```
/home/-UserName-/.wine
```
and
```
/root/.wine
```
and start everything all over (removing the .wine folder won't harm wine and it will be recreated the next time wine is run)
In your case, i understand you installed lotro inside:
```
/home/-UserName-/.wine/dosdevices/c:/Program Files/Codemasters/The Lord of the Rings Online/
```
You'd better relocate it before deleting /.wine/, and put it back inside when the folder is recreated.

Finally run your winetricks again:
```
$  winetricks.sh vcrun2005 vcrun2005sp1
```
That will install the dll you need AND the related service pack.
After that move, i was good to go..

---

### Post by ajackson on 2009-01-02
> **Bubblesix said:**
> I used to get a "failed to initialize" when, following the lotrolinux.com guide, I installed .NET 1.1 because of this statement:

### Post by Bubblesix on 2009-01-02
I am generally quite prudent, I tend to doubt about the fact I could gain anything installing a release deemed as "unstable" or "beta" (and even if, the advantages would be suppressed by the avalanche of disadvantages). 
This was not the case, luckily:
I decided to install the up-to-date ati drivers, and went for the "unstable" release of wine (wine 1.1.1), just to realize that did the trick.

The main improvement is that now, the text is completely readable, and comparable to the windows version (See image attached, compared to the older one taken with "stable" Wine 1.0.1).

Some thing just stood the same: 
-when windowed, the game still shows a black screen every time you clic with the mouse (just a flick) and when it's loading something (the world map, or any ingame window)
-The framerate is now the main issue:it is, just like before, not more than 10-15 fps in the best moments, independently from the graphic settings(even tried on minimal)

And finally, something went worse: the sound was clear before, while is now stuttering, giving me the impression my ministrel became some sort of bad house dj:(

The overall impression is good, though, and even if i'll still dual boot to play it seriously, I might give it a try as soon as a new wine (or drivers) come out.

### Post by ajackson on 2009-01-02
> **Bubblesix said:**
> I am generally quite prudent, I tend to doubt about the fact I could gain anything installing a release deemed as "unstable" or "beta"
Hey this is linux there is no such thing as stable, only varying degrees of instability :)

To be fair Wine does tend to stay pretty stable and has the advantage of being able to go back a version if the new one is not behaving itself.

> And finally, something went worse: the sound was clear before, while is now stuttering, giving me the impression my ministrel became some sort of bad house dj:(
That is a known problem between pulseaudio and wine, the best way of telling if it is pulseaudio causing the problem is to do a
```
pulseaudio -k
```
Before running the game (to stop pulseaudio from working, and then do a
```
pulseaudio -D
```
to restart pulseaudio when you quit the game.

With wine 1.1.11 and Jaunty the problem seems fixed but Jaunty is pre-release and not for the faint hearted. Wine 1.1.12 should have the fix in to stop the stutter but I can't test it any more on Intrepid as my Intrepid system no longer exists.

---

### Post by BatPenguin on 2009-01-03
> **monstrodoom said:**
> 
When trying to run lotrolauncher.script via the terminal, I'm told "permission denied." This is after keying in sudo and entering my password for root permission.

There's no need to run the script as sudo, dont' do it. Change the whole ~/.wine to be owned by you, make sure you've made the launcherscript executable (chomd a+x) and try again. Don't run the script as sudo - if you do, you'll end up having root be the owner of anything that gets modified.

Also make yourself the owner of ~/The Lord of the Rings Online and ~/.LotROLinux. If something doesn't work after changing the permissions, don't try to fix it by running it as sudo - post here with the new error message. Sudo will break it, the permissions error means just that - the permissions are wrong (root instead of user).

---

### Post by BatPenguin on 2009-01-08

Anyway, OSS sounds still don't work for me so I can't play by setting Winecfg to use OSS as I believe others are doing. But I've actually gotten the game to work with Alsa now, I suppose this could be of some interest to others here too. Not sure if it would with the default Ubuntu 8.10 Alsa as I have Pulseaudio ripped out now, but anyway, it works for me now as long as I use an /etc/asound.conf (ask me if interested in what it looks like) that just simply sets as my SPDIF out as the default output, no dmixer, and then I select Alsa in winecfg. If I do that, sound works: no sound in the first game screen (the "shwoosh") but after that all music and effects play normal. However, if I use a more normal setup with Dmix for mixing sounds, I get the first "swooosh" sound and then no sound after that. Strange but it works, so at least I can play.

So anyway: I'm left in the quite annoying setup now of having to change sound config files and reload Alsa whenever I want to switch between normal mixed sounds and the setup that works with Lotro (unmixed digital out). I believe this all could be solved by fixing up my OSS to use the digital out (problem is most likely that it's using analog outs) OR fixing my Aoss setup (Alsa's OSS support thing) to do the same, use digital. So then I could just set Winecfg to use OSS, like others and have Alsa mix sounds as before elsewhere. But I have very little idea of how to set that up. By googling and trying differnet things in /etc/asound.conf, I get System->Preferences to show various OSS output devices, but not my digital out, only analogs.

So please, if anybody here knows enough about Alsa/OSS and setting it up to work, especially with Wine, drop me a hint about what sort of /dev/dsp mysteries one has to write into /etc/asound.conf and/or whether pure OSS is setup somewhere else. This is probably just autoconfig magic that creates it all for most people, but not for my system. In short: if you are using a digital out and have OSS sound - or even better, Alsa - working in Wine/LoTRO, please show me what you have in your /etc/asound.conf and ~/asound* files. Or if you know how Wine can somehow be configured to truly use Dmixing and work, please let me know. As strange as it is, there's hardly anything written about setting up OSS (the old OSS, not the OSS4) anywhere, it's just apparently supposed to "just work" for everybody, so that's why I'm asking here for settings that demonstratably work in Wine/LoTRO. There's many threads about solving audio problems, but I haven't seen instructions for this. Thanks.

### Post by BatPenguin on 2009-01-10
Enigma83: Looks strange. Does changing other settings while Post-processing is on do anything? The glow settings etc...try putting every other setting all the way down or off first, then turn post-processing on, and see if it still does it. If it does, what display driver version are you using? There's apparently a Nvidia 1.80something beta avaiable, I was thinking of trying that to see if it would help with my High-quality texture crashes. 

Anyway: to conclude my monologue regarding audio issues, I finally figured/googled out how to set wine to use another audio device for Alsa. When I put this into my ~/.wine/user.reg, I get wine to use the right out for my Alsa sound:

```
[Software\\Wine\\Alsa Driver] 1240428288
"AutoScanCards"="N"
"DeviceCount"="2"
"DeviceCTL1"="dmixer"
"DevicePCM1"="hw:0,1"
"UseDirectHW"="N"
```

Doesn't seem that hard now but took only two weeks to find out for me, heh...Anyway, the important part is the DevicePCM1 which sets, manually, what output to use. Wine won't let you (or at least **me**) change it in any easier manner. Mixing still doesn't work, wine will grab the audio output for itself, but that's fine and "normal". These settings can of course be also done with Regedit and creating these keys.

Would be nice if Wine allowed you to just select the audio output you want from Winecfg...I should go see if there's a wine feature suggestion board somewhere and suggest that, it doesn't seem like it would be hard to do since you can just set it by altering a key like this, and with Pulseaudio now here, audio problems seem to be more common again, at least based on the amount of recent "how to fix your sound" threads here.

### Post by zami on 2009-01-11
Regarding VCRUN2005 and VCRUN2005sp1 -

I just wanted to share, when I tried installing the Vcruns via WineTricks, in Win2000 or WinXP mode, I kept having a problem where while installing, Vcrun would ask me 
"you must restart your machine in order to bla bla bla - restart now?"
Whether I said yay or nay, it would just error out.

I finally got it to install correctly while running Wine in Windows95 mode (that's right, 10 years Vcrun2005's senior!)  It didn't ask about restarting and just did it's job.  Then I switched back over to Win2000 mode, and finally, FINALLY LOTRO is updating (launching/patching via the CLI launcher)!  It's updated before but I know it was far too quick and the game would give me missing DLL error when I tried to launch.  

Anyhow, hopefully that can help if anyone runs into the same Vcrun problem I had.

-zami

### Post by creonnoir on 2009-01-11
I managed to get MoM working after many trial and error attempts.  Thanks to all who gave advice on this forum.  My particular situation seemed to be a combination of other's problems.

Specs: Ubuntu x64 8.10 on HP Pavilion dv5 laptop, most recent wine (1.1.12), nvidia 9600m gt with nvidia 180 drivers.

Steps:

Download the most recent MoM setup from LOTRO.
Grabbed the most recent winetricks script and set the script to execute.
Install the Lotrolauncher gui.
Ran the MoM setup first!  

Unfortunately the most recent installer installs some kind of incompatible vc2005 runtime, messing up the winetricks installation.
After the setup is complete, move the Turbine program folder from the .wine directory to another location (the desktop in my case).  

Delete the .wine directory, I don't have any other windows programs installed in my case, others have setup up a specific wine directory for MoM as detailed in previous messages.
Go to Applications->Wine->Configure Wine from the top menubar, this sets up a new .wine directory automatically.
Move the Turbine folder back under the Program Files directory.

From the command prompt:
```
sudo apt-get install cabextract
./winetricks
```

The winetricks command opens up a graphical menu, choose directx9, vcrun2005 and vcrun2005sp1 and then click ok.
Go to applications->games->The lord of the rings online.
Go to Tools->options and switch the game directory to the game directory.
Log in!

Everything worked well for me after this, the game ran smoothly with no problems and I didn't have to modify the registry or any other config files.

Hopefully this will help others who haven't been able to get the game working yet.

---

### Post by ajackson on 2009-01-23
For people on Intrepid (& Jaunty) who have been having problems with the gecko version crashing because of it not finding a library, try the following:

```
locate gecko-sharp.dll
```
Should give you /usr/lib/cli/gecko-sharp-2.0/gecko-sharp.dll

For some reason gecko-sharp.dll isn't being registered in Mono's central list of libraries (GAC), to register it type:

```
sudo gac -i /usr/lib/cli/gecko-sharp-2.0/gecko-sharp.dll
```

It should tell you that gecko-sharp is now installed and the gecko version of LotROLinux should work fine now.

That being said, I get crashes as well, but they only happen when high-quality textures are in use and usually when loading new areas - sometimes when running around but even then it's normally a moment where there's some major changes between scenery going on. The only graphics option that affects these crashes for me is the texture quality. When I set it to High instead of the best (very high? I forget what it's called), I never get crashes. Everything else is pretty much maxed except for some shadow setting that made the ground dark in places.

> **Vaitya said:**
> As well, is there any easy way to kill a fullscreen program running in wine? My only successes have been spamming between screen 1 and screen 2 so far.

I guess this is what you do (maybe?) but I just switch to another desktop by pressing cltrl+alt+arrow and open a terminal, or usually I have one open anyway, and do "sudo killall lotroclient.exe".

Sounds strange that the game is trying to use a graphics mode that your monitor doesn't support, weird. I've never actually seen stuff like that with LCDs, used to be more common with the old type of monitors.

Anyway, here's my UserPreferences.ini file. Copy the text, save it as ~/The Lord of the Rings Online/UserPreferences.ini and edit your normal desktop resolution into the resolution line. If this doesn't work, make sure your winecfg doesn't have the wine virtual desktop (or whatever it's called) enabled and set to some strangely small resolution. I hope this help.

My UserPreferences.ini:


```

[Display]

Resolution=1360x768

SyncToRefresh=False

Antialiasing=Disabled

WindowedResolution=800x600

TripleBuffering=True

FullScreen=True

AllowDesktopCompositing=False

AllowWindowResizing=True

AllowFakeFullScreen=True

ConfineFullScreenMouseCursor=True

RefreshRate=Auto

ForceFakeFullScreen=False



[Render]

GraphicsCore=D3D9

VideoPostEffects=True

AnimationDetail=High

AllowGammaChanges=True

TextureFiltering=VerySharp

Gamma=2.18

AnisotropicQuality=4

MaxHardwareClass=0

DisplayAdapter=0

Contrast=0.96

Brightness=0.93

AlphaToCoverage=False

PlayerCrowdQuality=0.00

BlobShadows=False

OverbrightBloomFilter=True

TextureDetail=High

LandscapeShoreEffects=High

MemoryUsage=0.00

LandscapeStaticObjectShadows=Low

EnablePortraits=True

BlurFilterQuality=High

EnvironmentStencilShadows=True

FarLandscapeNormalMaps=False

HavePromptedForD3D10AtStartup=False



[Net]

BindInterface=

TurnKeySpeed=150.00

### Post by SNy on 2009-01-27
> **Vaitya said:**
> I have the mic working in the desktop X environment, but notta in LOTRO. IN gae when I go to the audio menu the Voice Playback Device listed is Realtek ALC662 REV1, while there are no options to select under Voice Capture Device.

Sound seems to be a somewhat common problem, I believe BatPenguin also does have some issues there. I think it stems from the fact that there are so many options for sound in general. Different types of hardware, different sound architectures and stuff like dmix and alsa-oss on top make this very tedious to figure out.

FWIW, my sound output works fine for ingame and video cut-scenes (I don't even get the "swoosh" starting animation under wine, so can't comment on sound there). I also can use my microphone in game, but ONLY if I don't start other software that wants to use it (ie TeamSpeak). So if I start TS first, I can use that juse fine but have no mic setting in game, just like you. I didn't have the nerve to fiddle with that once I got it to that working state. :P

HTH,
SNy

---

### Post by BatPenguin on 2009-01-27
> **SNy said:**
> Sound seems to be a somewhat common problem, I believe BatPenguin also does have some issues there. I think it stems from the fact that there are so many options for sound in general. Different types of hardware, different sound architectures and stuff like dmix and alsa-oss on top make this very tedious to figure out.

Yes, I had major problems with sound too. Might help a bit to look up my previous posts here, I think I documented pretty well what was broken and what I did to "fix" it. I haven't used a microphone with Lotro though, so I can't help you there. But maybe just setting up ALSA (no pulse) could get you somewhere, if the microphone would work under normal ALSA. A word of warning though: getting rid of pulseaudio completely is not recommended and might break your system sound for good. I had to do it and everything works better now than before, but it might just completely break sound from your system. There's plenty of how-tos and discussions about Pulseaudio - most of them about people complaining how it doesn't work and others claiming it's the best thing ever...guess which side of the argument I'm on :) 

So yes, for me, the fix for sound issues was uninstalling pulseaudio. I don't actually think that's necessary for most people, since you can just kill pulseaudio and then try to use ALSA directly normally, so that's all I can suggest now: try killing pulseaudio and then set Wine to use ALSA and see if you can get your mic working. If you get it working, backup your config files and write down somewhere (this board is a good place) how you got it to work. The chances are that the next "update" to something or other will break it once more...:D

SNY's comment about the order in which you start programs is very important by the way, I think Wine always insists on grabbing the audio hardware for itself so make sure it's available. Good luck.

---

### Post by BatPenguin on 2009-01-27
> **hexdsl said:**
> has anyone got any ideas on my DX problem (see last post) would my graphics drivers be anything to do with this? (any good tutorials on updating them?) 

hope some one can help me soon, dont want to live in a world without hobbits

For a quite recent driver, just look for nvidia-glx-180 in Synaptic and install it and enable the restricted nvidia driver. That should do it (I'd backup xorg.conf anyway just in case).

You said you've followed the guides: does this include the winetricks install? Did it install fine and the game patch itself all the way? And have you set the video memory correctly in regedit?

Those are probably the most common reasons for failures recently (especially the winetricks / vcrun thing). The error does sound like a graphics card / driver problem, so could be that you just have to make sure you're using the proper restricted driver or reinstall it.

Among my e-mails this morning was a notice about changes being made to the PPA server which hosts the LotROLinux repository. Basically the address is changing (though the old one will work for a month or so as well) and the PPA is now authenticated.

### Post by BatPenguin on 2009-01-29
> **hexdsl said:**

Are you using Desktop effects? Try switching those off. Also: do you have video memory setup in regedit? (feel like I've been asking this question every day from somebody for the past two weeks now...;)

Also: are you trying to run it in a window? Look in your ~/The Lord of the Rings Online/UserPreferences.ini and make sure you're running it in fullscreen. I'd set the resolution to the same one you use normally for your desktop too.

### Post by BatPenguin on 2009-01-29
> **hexdsl said:**
> okay, can confirm that without desktop effects turned on it stalls at same place but does not 'grey out' still stalls though, same problem but without desktop effect crash dont look as nice :(

any more ideas?

Just one: Do you have the 180.22 drivers installed? ([http://www.nvidia.com/object/linux_display_ia32_180.22.html](http://www.nvidia.com/object/linux_display_ia32_180.22.html))

That driver apparently adds support to your card, 9500 GT. So make sure you're using that exact version. Look on the forums somewhere for 180.xx series drivers discussion, I'm sure you'll find instructions on how to get those installed nicely. I'm currently using the 180.11, not sure if you can find the 180.22 in a nice repository or not. If not, do it the old fashioned way and break your graphics by installing the driver from the Nvidia website :) Should be plenty of instructions elsewhere here.

About the desktop effects: I recommend playing without them, at least for me they just add more glitches to games and videos, I have them off for these sorts of things. For a nice way of switching them off and automatically back when you exit the program, browse through my messages here - I posted about a neat trick called compizswitcher here, umm, some time ago. I use it for all my games, video programs and Mythtv. Should find it by looking up compizswitcher from this thread, I think I put a link up to the proper thread about how to do it.

Now I can see the MoM splash screens. Can't get any farther than that yet, but I think I'll eventually get it to run. I hope... :)

Glad you got that far now, good work. By the way, I did a quick look at your previous posts here (to see what card you have, you haven't mentioned it) and noticed that you'd posted something about wine and setting graphics driver names (some .dll question) previously in another thread...didn't look too closely at what that was about, but if you have some funky other Direct3D etc. settings for Wine for another game, get rid of them or run Lotro separately from these other settings. You really don't have to do anything other than set the video memory size for Lotro for Wine, so it's best to start from a clean wine install. Something that's needed for one game can break another.

And of course, make sure you have the latest driver...you do have an Nvidia card, right? Seeing the ATI slideshow is really not worth the trouble. Good luck.

Yes on all counts: GeForce 8800GT, newest driver, and lots of registry settings from other games. You're absolutely right. I need to run from a clean wine install. I'm pretty sure that's what's causing the problem, as I used to be able to run LotRO with no issues on this machine. I'll give it a try when I have some time to spare and report back. 

Thanks for the heads-up.

P.S. That .dll setting for the registry is required in order to run Fallout 3 in Wine. Basically, F3 won't recognize that you have a good enough graphics card to run it and will refuse to start unless you tell it via the registry. Not sure why I would have brought that up in a LotRO thread, but then I don't know why I do a lot of things...

[B]The *new* copy, in .wine-lotro, fails during the authentication process. It can't grab the "world queue configuration," for whatever reason. I guess it needs that in order to patch, because there's no "Patch" option in the Tools menu, either.

### Post by zami on 2009-02-02
> **jnewl said:**
> Actually, I've been avoiding bringing this up here since I was afraid it would muck up the works with too much complication, but I actually have two different installs going. Here's the picture:

I have a copy of LotRO I bought when it first came out, plus another copy that was bought for me when Mines of Moria was released. Since I had already successfully played the original copy on my machine, that's the one I installed in the .wine directory. However, I followed all the directions at WineHQ for installing my new, Mines of Moria copy in another directory, .wine-lotro, using WINEPREFIX and all that. I followed the instructions to the letter. 

So now I have two installations, but neither one is working yet, and for two different reasons. The old copy, which is in .wine, gives me a refresh rate out of range error when I try to launch it fullscreen, and so far I haven't been able to get past the splash screens when running windowed. 

[B]The *new* copy, in .wine-lotro, fails during the authentication process. It can't grab the "world queue configuration," for whatever reason. It sees the game servers and reports those properly, but whatever other data it needs after that it can't find. I guess it needs that in order to patch, because there's no "Patch" option in the Tools menu, either. 
[/B]
Bottom line: if you can give me some guidance on getting this latter copy to launch (forgetting the other copy entirely for the moment), I may be all set. I'm not sure where to go with it at this point.


Can I ask, for the 2nd directory, wine-lotro, are you POSITIVE you've installed the vcrun stuff correctly?  For whatever reason, when I installed it, the first time it appeared to install correctly, but I kept having problems that others suggested were related to Vcrun.  (No action or hints using the GUI launcher, and .dll errors using the CLI launcher.)  When I tried to reinstall the Vcruns, I'd get a message about needing to restart Windows, and then wintricks would just give a fuzzy message.  I finally ran Wine in Windows95 mode and Vcrun installed with no problems.  Switched back to Win2000 mode, and the game finally updated and launched correctly.

If you haven't already, you might want to try the CLI launcher.  It's not as convenient as the GUI launcher, but you can see the output when it has problems.  The GUI should too I guess... it' an option to log any errors, but for me it just never logged anything.  Now that the game is running fine, I've gone back the GUI for launching the game, but I'll be using the CLI for any future troubleshooting.

-zami

### Post by monstrodoom on 2009-02-06
[You'll probably want to refer to this.](http://appdb.winehq.org/objectManager.php?sClass=version&iId=14566) It's handy instructions to stably configuring a Wine prefix for running the game.

I'm not familiar with Crossover Games, like at all. I run the game through an exported prefix of Wine so my Wine configuration stays stable as I up- or down-grade it; I imagine most users here do the same. I know AJackson's page has something referring to Crossover but I think it's more oriented to Mac users?

Anyway, the game runs so smoothly for me I'm not even concerned about the FPS rates I'm getting; about the only time I get any slowdown is in raid-vs-raid combat in Ettenmoors. I suspect it may be the utility you're using.

### Post by BatPenguin on 2009-02-11
> **AZbigbear said:**
> Brand new to Ubuntu, and no idea what to do to get the GUI to compile. Never used the terminal before, so could anyone help?

I have the Tarbell downloaded and mono installed and wine installed. Just need to somehow figure out compiling so i can get the GUI to work.

No need to compile anything, just add ajackson's repository and install with Synaptic. Instructions here: [http://www.lotrolinux.com/download.php](http://www.lotrolinux.com/download.php) . Look through all of the installation instructions, though, there's a few other steps included as well. Be sure to install vcrun with winetricks and set the needed registry entries, those should be the most common problems that prevent patching or the game running.

Ajackson's page should have the link to instructions and most problems have probably been dealt with in this forum many times so if you have problems, the thread search function is probably very handy. And of course we'll help, if you need it. Good luck!

### Post by BatPenguin on 2009-02-12
> **AZbigbear said:**
> Ok I found what i need in Synaptic, thanks for the help.

But now after I load up the Launcher and try to launch into the game, the launcher goes away but thats it.

You probably just need to patch the game. Options -> Patch in the launcher. If that doesn't work, you have to install winetricks/vcrun for real. Use the search.

If you still have problems, please describe **all the steps **you have done in your next post including how you installed the game. You're being quite vague in these posts, please clarify them: I saw that you originally wrote in this reply here that you already have winetricks and vcrun installed and then removed that bit of very useful information for some reason. Please tell us more, not less, of what you have done if you continue to have problems.

If you describe exactly what you have done, how you installed the game (original DVD?) and in what order and what error messages you get on your screen or in the log file in ~/The Lord of the Rings Online, I'd say the odds are pretty good that one of us will know exactly what you need to do to get it going. I'm also unsure how you can have winetricks installed when you just wrote that you've "never used the terminal before". You've also not mentioned anything about your system: you need an Nvidia card and a recent driver to play it. If patching doesn't work now, please write a proper description of everything you've done including graphics card / driver version info. I'll gladly help and I don't mean to be rude, but please, let's not waste everybody's time asking all these questions one at a time.

### Post by klipart on 2009-02-16
> **creonnoir said:**
> I managed to get MoM working after many trial and error attempts.  Thanks to all who gave advice on this forum.  My particular situation seemed to be a combination of other's problems.

Specs: Ubuntu x64 8.10 on HP Pavilion dv5 laptop, most recent wine (1.1.12), nvidia 9600m gt with nvidia 180 drivers.

Steps:

Download the most recent MoM setup from LOTRO.
Grabbed the most recent winetricks script and set the script to execute.
Install the Lotrolauncher gui.
Ran the MoM setup first!  

Unfortunately the most recent installer installs some kind of incompatible vc2005 runtime, messing up the winetricks installation.
After the setup is complete, move the Turbine program folder from the .wine directory to another location (the desktop in my case).  

Delete the .wine directory, I don't have any other windows programs installed in my case, others have setup up a specific wine directory for MoM as detailed in previous messages.
Go to Applications->Wine->Configure Wine from the top menubar, this sets up a new .wine directory automatically.
Move the Turbine folder back under the Program Files directory.

From the command prompt:
```
sudo apt-get install cabextract
./winetricks
```

The winetricks command opens up a graphical menu, choose directx9, vcrun2005 and vcrun2005sp1 and then click ok.
Go to applications->games->The lord of the rings online.
Go to Tools->options and switch the game directory to the game directory.
Log in!

Everything worked well for me after this, the game ran smoothly with no problems and I didn't have to modify the registry or any other config files.

Hopefully this will help others who haven't been able to get the game working yet.

You sir are the man, I have spent a few days trying to figure out how to get this game to work getting rundll32.exe errors when trying to patch it, and I tried what you suggested and it worked like a charm.

 I however am having sound issues. I understand that wine has a pulseaudio compatibility problem. I have an old Sound Blaster Live! that I have tweaked into working (rather well considering how old it is).

### Post by BatPenguin on 2009-02-19
> **Ravenwing said:**
> Alright. First things first. You guys are awesome and you get big props for getting everything working.

 I however am having sound issues. I understand that wine has a pulseaudio compatibility problem. I have an old Sound Blaster Live! that I have tweaked into working (rather well considering how old it is).

 Ok so no sound in the game. I get sound during the Turbine splash screen but nothing after. I check the sound settings at the character selection screen I can get the sound to "pop" when I hit the mute toggle. 

 So for some reason sound is getting piped in but gets muted or turned off for some reason.

 On another note.. Trying to pipe alsa threw pulseaudio has no effect. 

 Anyways... any "out of the box" ideas are most welcome.

I got the same "whoosh but nothing else" thing at some point of my quest against Ubuntu sound systems. You should probably look up my posts on this forum about sound (use the thread search), I posted a few times about my problems and what I did to solve them.

In any case, Pulseaudio sounds like the problem for you, so I suggest you try the following (in this order):

- Use winecfg to change sound system to OSS and try the game. If sound works, great. (just in case it'd work like this, just try it)
- If not, change back to ALSA in winecfg and kill pulseaudio: "sudo killall pulseaudio". Load ALSA again "sudo alsa force-reload". Then try the game.

If killing Pulseaudio works for you, just set up a launch script that does that for you whenever you launch the game. I don't recommend doing what I did (read the posts) and wiping Pulseaudio as it's a pretty well integrated part of Ubuntu now, but that's what I had to do. There are nicer ways of simply disabling pulseaudio, but well, don't get too creative. Sound is incredibly hard to fix once you properly break it :)

Speaking of which -- now my sound is broken once more, for some reason my dmix sound mixing stopped working (no settings were changed) and now I'm back to having ALSA sound in one application at a time. I think I'll just give up on my sound hardware, this might be a hardware issue too, and disable it and buy a PCI / PCI-e card. If anybody has a suggestion of a card like that (that does digital output to a receiver nicely, any PCI/PCI-e sound card you might have that JUST WORKS with 8.10 would be great), please oh please let me know. Browsing the ALSA project pages trying to locate a card that does what you want -- and hoping it'll wokr with pulseaudio -- is not very much fun at all (trust me, been doing that for an evening or two now).

### Post by ajackson on 2009-02-19
If you don't mind waiting a couple of months PulseAudion 0.9.14 is in Jaunty and works very nice with wine via Alsa.

There is an experimental pulseaudio plugin for wine but it means that you need to compile wine to use it. There are three parts to it (comments 50, 51 & 61) in [http://bugs.winehq.org/show_bug.cgi?id=10495](http://bugs.winehq.org/show_bug.cgi?id=10495)

I haven't got round to testing it yet so I don't know how good/bad it is. Another thing to possible try is finding a repository that has PulseAudio 0.9.14 for Intrepid, I don't know of one off hand but there probably is one somewhere.

### Post by BatPenguin on 2009-02-20
> **Ravenwing said:**

I killed Pulse and restarted alsa and got here is what the output was:

-------------------------------------------------------------------------
 My Ubuntu-Fu is not this strong. How do I fix this issue?

That output looks like what I get. It unloads the modules and reloads then and throws in some weird gv whatever  error for flavor (it doesn't seem to be important, I googled it at some point). Alsa should be working then, did you try the game after that? load up gmome-alsamixer or alsamixer and make sure your volume is not muted and try the game. Oh oops...just remembered this: open Preferences - Sound, set everything to alsa and see if you get the test sound. If not, try the otherf options you might have there, stuff like ALC888 digital etc. show up for me, see if something will play test sound when pulse is killed and alsa eloaded. But yes, set pref-sound to alsa and try. Forgot this earlier.

THIS is the easy way of getting around pulse with wine. Wine will use alsa so by doing this (killing pulse temporarily) you are going back to pretending that pulse isn't there.

If this really wont work for you, read my other sound posts, perhaps messing with your asound.conf file is needed. What is your sound hardware anyway?

Oh and ajackson: thanks for the link. Interesting stuff about pulse/wine...odd to see the hostility toward official wine support for pulse. Even though it's never worked for me, I see its benefits and am truly happy if Linux sound starts to standardize by adopting it more widely. Just gotta get a new card...

Edit: Oh yes -- and when you try Lotro after killing pulse etc, make sure to go into at least the character selection screen to see if sound works. For me, the "swoosh" and video sounds don't work but music and sound effects do. So if you don't hear the logo sound, that doesn't necessarily mean anything yet, I don't get it either.

### Post by BatPenguin on 2009-02-20
> **Ravenwing said:**
> ok. I dumped Pulse and reloaded alsa and got the same issue. Alsa still loads (like you said) so that isn't and issue. Check 1

 Playing around in my sound settings on the game I noticed (running wine with ALSA not OSS) capture was on "Generic Software on ADC Capture/Standard PCM Playback"

 Ok. PCM is not muted and volume is all the way up. So I check my sound settings.

SB Live 5.1 (rev.7,serial:0x80641102) ADC Capture/Standard PCM Playback (ALSA)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
audiotestsrc wave=sine freq=512 ! audioconvert ! audioresample ! gconfaudiosink: Could not open audio device for playback.
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
However I do get sound and the tone on the 
SB Live 5.1 (rev.7,serial:0x80641102) Multichannel Playback (ALSA)


So problem found? This is the first I have run into this issue. Does your asoundrc.conf file have something to do with this? And/Or can I have Lotro select the Multichannel rather then the PCM? No option for the multichannel in the game.

Yes, there is a way to change what ALSA uses, here's my post about that:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=6525709&postcount=1746](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=6525709&postcount=1746)

If you get sound fron Multichannel, I suggest you do a "aplay -l" and/or "aplay -L" or some other arcane ALSA magic and figure out what number that output is, and then try to use that in that wine file (numbering starts with 0,1,2...). Or just start from 0 and work your way up until you either find it or, well, give up. Another confusing thing is the mixer...I had dmix setup for a while, like in that post, and then it just mysteriously broke, so frankly, I'm not much of an authority to say what'll work there, perhaps "default". But that's anyway the way to specify what ALSA will use. Play around, hopefully that helps you set it to something that works.

If you need to edit the /etc/asound.conf file, the system-wide settings file (the ~/.asound* files deal with your personal account's settings), what worked and still works for me is directing it all to the digital out:

```

pcm.!default {
  type plug
  slave {
    pcm "spdif"
    rate 48000
    format S16_LE
  }
}

```

By doing this, I didn't have to set the alsa device like above since it's all default (nothing's mixed thouhg, one application at a time).

But this asound.conf file is all probably quite dependent on hardware. Since your card is a pretty common one, I think (used to have one too), the ALSA pages could be helpful to you for setting it up for alsa. Sound settings are extremely dependant on the card, so go look at the [www.alsa-project.org](www.alsa-project.org) pages and your card there if no "easy" fix works.

Again, I'm talking about pure alsa here since Pulse doesn't work for me. So if you try these alsa things, you probably should kill pulse first before trying them. Although as far as I understand it, pulseaudio should "just work" with alsa if it is setup correctly.

### Post by Ravenwing on 2009-02-21
Ok... Here is how I fixed my sound on the laptop not the PC.

 I switched to windowed mode (used the in game settings)
 Had wine disallow any window control for the program
 chose EsounD Driver
       Full hardware acceleration
       48000 sample rate
       16 bps
       no driver emulation

### Post by ajackson on 2009-02-21
> **Ravenwing said:**

Have a look in your userprefs file for

SoundProvider=
```

If it's not set to OpenAL try changing it to that, see if it makes any difference.

> There has to be something screwing with the sound for it to be picky enough to have videos but not ingame sounds or music.
I can remember when we were first getting it working it used to be you could get in-game sounds but not for the cut scenes, it seemed so sound card dependent, playing around with the wine sound settings would eventually get both working **but** people with identical sound cards could often end up with different settings as the other persons settings didn't work for them.

> Perhaps both Alsa and Pulse will be improved along with the new distro of Ubuntu.
In Jaunty Alsa and Pulse (and Wine) just seem to get along without any fiddling, I might just be lucky with my sound setup but I haven't had any issues with Wine being silent (except for the normal problems you can get when the OS is still in pre-release).

### Post by Ravenwing on 2009-02-21
ok a good night sleep and a fresh mind often help us to see the obvious...

 Here is how I fixed my sound in LotRO (on my PC)
 Sound Blaster Live! 5.1 (Alsa)

 .asoundrc file
```

 pcm.!default {
    type pulse
}
ctl.!default {
    type pulse
}
load-module module-alsa-sink device=hw:0
load-module module-alsa-source device=hw:0

```

Wine regedit settings
 ```

[HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Wine\Alsa Driver]

"AutoScanCards"="N"
"UseDirectHW"="N"

```

Wine Settings: 
```

[HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Wine\AppDefaults\lotroclient.exe]
"Version"="win2003"

[HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Wine\AppDefaults\lotroclient.exe\DirectSound]
"DefaultSampleRate"="48000"
"EmulDriver"="N"

[HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Wine\AppDefaults\lotroclient.exe\Explorer]

[HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Wine\AppDefaults\lotroclient.exe\X11 Driver]
"Decorated"="N"
"Managed"="N"

```

UserPreferences.ini:
```

[Sound]
AmbientVolume=1.00
SoundVolume=1.00
DMTextVolume=1.00
SoundProvider=OpenAL
CombatVolume=1.00
MaxPlayingSounds=64
MusicVolume=0.70
SoundFeatures=Generic Software on default
UIVolume=1.00
EAXEnabled=False
PlayerMusicVolume=1.00
VOVolume=1.00
SoundDisabled=False
MusicDisabled=False

[Display]
AllowFakeFullScreen=False
ForceFakeFullScreen=False
FullScreen=False

```

 Now this works for me with the game in windowed mode. The sound WILL NOT WORK in fullscreen for some reason that boggles my mind. I have to go threw a small dance once the game launches to get the screen to stick so I can use the mouse but other then that its music to my ears.

 I hope this will help someone else who has been having issues with their sound who has the same card as me. Or at the very least gives them the ability to hear some sound at all.

 Cheers guys. On to getting my DDO up and running!

---

### Post by CSWScrooge on 2009-02-22
Hello everyone,

I recently upgraded my system with the one of the currently two choices for dx10 (what for? you might ask. Well I have my reasons ;)) agp graphics (at least around my home). Sadly, it's an ATI HD 3850.

Therefore, I just wanted to check on the status of the ATI drivers and LoTRO. So I installed the latest Catalyst 9.2 suite from AMD/ATI and tried to run the client, which, amazingly, worked at once (well, with low fps between 2 and 20 of course).

I searched the web for tweaks and came upon something like this (adapted from original WOW-Tweak found [here]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=303509&page=1") and put into lotrolauncher.script):
```

gksudo X :3 -ac&
sleep 10
export WINEDEBUG=fixme-all
DISPLAY=:3 wine ${gameClient_FILE} ${gameClient_ARGS} $@
```

This is supposed to start a separate X server on screen 3 and runs wine lotroclient.exe on that screen. I don't know what that would actually accomplish but some wow-gamers have reported significant fps improvements.

For me it currently doesn't work, because I'm using evdev, which causes some trouble with that 'tweak'. I'm going to try it without evdev, but currently I'm a bit busy, so that may take a while.

However, I would like to know if anyone else with an ATI/AMD card tried that already or is willing to try it out and to report the results in the meantime.

> **Devilman13 said:**

The only quirk I'm having is that the npc quest/shop icons which should appear on each model are black boxes or black ountlines of rings. Things such as leaves falling in Michel Delving are also black, but not boxes. I can also see the glowing effect of an icon behind a box in some cases.

Has anyone else encountered this bug and know how to fix it?
