[[IMG]http://img15.imageshack.us/img15/8193/lotroblackspots.th.png[/IMG]](http://img15.imageshack.us/my.php?image=lotroblackspots.png)

Yes, I've seen that. And no, I don't remember how I fixed it :) If you've recently updated your Nvidia driver, it could be that...I'm using the latest 180.29 and I don't have that problem now, but it could've been that that was something on the day when I went through a few nvidia versions (180.06 - 180.11 etc.) while playing around.

But I don't think it was about the driver actually (just mentioning in case you have updated recently), I believe it was shadows or some other setting like that, so I suggest you go to a spot where you get that effect (near a merchant etc.), make a note of your current shadow settings, turn them all off, restart the game (some of these shadow settings seem to need that so do that) and then - if the effect is gone - start turning shadow settings on one by one a little bit at a time. If it wasn't shadows it was some other graphics setting, bloom etc....in any case, you should be able to locate what's causing that by turning everything down and then working your way up. It's fixable.

You have WINEPREFIX set to .wine-lotro in your home directory but the image of your LOTRO directory shows that LOTRO is installed in .wine, unless your LOTRO is installed in a Wine setup other than the default .wine you can leave WINEPREFIX blank.

### Post by ajackson on 2009-03-12
> **smckenzie said:**
> I am fairly new to linux myself, (will never go back) and am trying to figure  out which file for LOTRO is the main game file, i have never realy used WINE before so i just need to know how to find the file to launch through the LOTROLinux launcher.
All you need to do is ensure that the directory points to the directory that contains lotroclient.exe, if the realm list gets filled then you know you have the right place (assuming that the servers are up of course).

> I also recieve a Microsoft runtime error when attempting to launch assuming i am even launching the proper file....I hope that made some kind of sense.
I've recently re-installed LotRO to give it another go and I hit that problem. It's caused by the .NET 2 installation, something about that is upsetting Wine (but LotRO installs .NET 2 while installing itself).

The easy fix is to rename your .wine directory, create a new one, install the Visual C++ 2005 run-time & service pack 1 and then move your LotRO install over.

Or in command terms (first two steps deals with getting winetricks, skip if you already have it)

Run in your home directory
```

wget http://kegel.com/wine/winetricks
chmod +x winetricks
mv .wine .wine-backup
winecfg
(change version to windows 2000 & close)
./winetricks vcrun2005 vcrun2005sp1
cd .wine/drive_c/Prog*
mv ~/.wine-backup/drive_c/Prog*/Turbine .

```

NB for European users replace Turbine with Codemasters

That should do the job of effectively removing .NET 2 and allowing the game to launch, get rid of .wine-backup, type the following to be prompted to install gecko under wine
```
wine iexplore http://www.google.com
```

Then try the launcher again. It should (hopefully) patch & run this time.

A better fix will be adding a registry entry to stop it installing .NET 2 (like we used to have to with .NET 1.1 but I'm still working on that).

I am doing a replacement for LotROLinux that uses python, it will tidy up of the things that have led to confusion in the past (ie what settings you need to alter in the settings window) and make capturing the various stuff pumped out by patching and running the game a lot easier (basically redirecting the terminal output into a window). That is work in progress though.

> As I am brand new to WINE, it is frustrating, because i know people are getting LOTRO to work just fine, and I obviously want to as well but am not......(frustrating). Please be patient
Don't worry, LotRO is one of those awkward games to get working as you have to do some fiddling. I might see if I can writing a prep utility that sets up the wine directory before hand (since winetricks can do a lot of what is needed for me), so that installing should be fairly pain-free.

---

### Post by ajackson on 2009-03-13
OK I think I've worked it out, there are two scenarios:

1) Completely fresh wine install - Just run the installer and let it do it's stuff, no need to install the VC2005 runtimes as it installs what is needed. The only thing you may need to do is the wine iexplore [http://www.google.com](http://www.google.com) to get gecko set up. I did get an error pop up during the install but clicking OK got rid of it and the game patched/started fine.

2) Updating existing LotRO setup. This is where you need to install the VC2005 runtimes but because .NET 2.0 isn't installed there is no conflict between it and the VC2005 installation.

I do strongly recommend having a separate wine directory/cxg bottle for LotRO though as it seems very easy to upset.

### Post by ajackson on 2009-03-13
> **smckenzie said:**
> I am not quite sure of what to do from here, once goole pops up within wine then what?, search for gecko and install it? I apologize again it is the n00b in me....;-)
Oops should have explained that bit a bit better, when google pops up it means that wine has configured its Internet Explorer substitute, LotRO might not need it any more as they switched the in-game help system to something a bit more non-Windows friendly a while back.

Just close the IE window and give the laucher another go, the patching window might hang if you are doing a big update but forcing it to quit doesn't hurt anything and if there were more patches to be applied it would pick them up when you fire the patcher off again.

Hopefully you should then be able to start the game.

### Post by ajackson on 2009-03-14
> **smckenzie said:**
> **Second** - once i get the confirmation that account has been authenticated the launcher closes as if to begin running the game, the screen goes black as though it is launching, then changes the screen res. then just shows my desktop.
What version of Wine are you using (type wine --version in a terminal window) as that sounds like an old problem that used to exists. The fix is to look in your home directory, there should be a folder called Lord of the Rings Online, in there is a file called UserPreferences.ini. Edit that file and find the line that says AllowFakeFullScreen. Change that to False (don't know if the capital F is required but I always include it).

> **Third** -  been repeating steps 1-2....lol so no actual game launch as of yet. now you had mentioned something about getting rid of .wine_backup
All .wine-backup contains is your previous install of LotRO with the actual game files removed, it doesn't hurt anything being there but it is basically a waste of disk space so can easily be deleted.

> how do i exaclty go about "getting rid of it" through CLI? 
If you followed the steps of backing up your existing Wine directory and creating a new one you now have a Wine install that doesn't contain .NET 2.0.

> 2 - click on + HKCU > + Software > R click on "Wine" > new key, named it Direct3D press enter. Right click on Direct3D new string value then copy in "**HKEY_CURRENT_USER/Software/Wine/Direct3D/VideoMemorySize = 256**" as my vid card is 256. press enter 
Close, right click on Direct3D, select new string and type VideoMemorySize, hit enter, double click on that entry in the right hand pane and type 256 in the little box that pops up.

You can get rid of your incorrect entry by right clicking on it and one of the options should be delete.

You also might want to pop over to [http://www.winehq.org/](http://www.winehq.org/) and follow the links and instructions on the download page for getting the latest version of wine for Ubuntu as the one in the repositories is pretty old.

### Post by wylfing on 2009-03-17
(EDIT: Consolidating my triple-post into a single problem-solution monologue.)

Here is the progression of my multi-hour journey from not being able to install all the way to having the game running perfectly.

**Problem** - 10-Day Trial installer crashes quite early in the install process.
**Solution** - Use the full Mines of Moria installer. You can find it by clicking the Support link on the lotro.com main page. In all other respects I followed [these instructions]("http://lorebook.lotro.com/wiki/LOTRO_under_Linux_and_Mac_OS/X"). Then I installed the [lotrolinux launcher]("http://lotrolinux.com/"), started the launcher, chose Tools > Options, and set it to the game's install directory.

UPDATE: I just performed an install on a Hardy box (running wine 1.1.17). I used only a clean .wine and the MoM installer. No "preparation" like fakeNET11 or winetricks. The installation seems to be successful. So the Lorebook instructions I linked immediately above may not be necessary any more.

**Problem** - Trying to play the game from the launcher produced...nothing. The launcher just disappeared. I expected it to crash the first time through, yet still create the necessary config files. It crashed, but didn't make any files. Additional attempts to run the game crashed the same way.
**Solution** - Patch, dummy! From the launcher, Tools > Patch and click Apply. It took a veeerrrry long time to patch. At one point (while patching sound.dat) it actually died. Just kill everything, re-run the launcher, and re-run the patch procedure. After fully patching, I got a slightly different result.

**Problem** - Trying to play the game from the launcher produced a black screen for 1 second, then crash back to the desktop. Still no config files being created either.
**Solution** - Followed [these instructions]("http://ubuntu-ky.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=6536119#post6536119"). Other people/posts have said essentially the same thing (i.e., make a new .wine and move your installed files over), I point out this one only because it's exactly the one I followed.

At this point I was able to launch the game and it plays pretty much flawlessly. There are a few visual quirks with the floating text, but not a big deal.

---

There's the issue with the black vendor icons which hasn't been solved, but if you're having issues with floating texts flashing behind and in front of people., there's an option somewhere in Lotro's Options for "floating text always front" or something like that. That fixed all floating text issues for me. Not sure if this is what you meant, but figured it's worth mentioning.

### Post by ajackson on 2009-03-28
The game mysteriously black screening then crashing on start up could be caused by an unimplemented function in the wine version of d3dx9_36.dll. If you just ran the installer and it did the directx step then you should be ok as a native windows version will exist. If directx did not install (ie you copied an existing setup or something) then you need to get a copy of d3dx9_36.dll (google for it) and stick it in the game directory. That solved the problem for me.

---

### Post by ajackson on 2009-03-31
If you do a ```
wine --version
``` from a terminal does it show you the wine version, if so which version is it?

If it if 1.0.1 (or something similar) pop over to [http://www.winehq.com](http://www.winehq.com) and follow the steps to install their Ubuntu repository (so you have the latest wine).

The main drawback of LotROLinux is that the logging of output from patching/running isn't that brilliant. For now there's not much you can do (enable wine output and see what ~/.LotROLinux/run.log contains maybe) but the replacement for LotROLinux is nearing the stage where I can let others loose with it and the capturing of output from the wine calls is a lot better.

Edit: Just spotted that LOTRO is installed in .wine-lotro, that is the problem (well the cause of the problem). In the options window for WINEPREFIX type in /home/username/.wine-lotro (where username is your username, I apologise if you worked that out yourself but I have had a few funny PMs/emails where people have typed username :))

### Post by wylfing on 2009-03-31
martialartist81,

This sounds like exactly the problem I was having. You need to do a little shuffling-around of files to make things work. Follow the instructions in *[this link]("http://ubuntu-ky.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=6536119#post6536119")*, reposted below for convenience. 

EDIT: It refers to .wine below. If you have LoTRO installed under .wine-lotro, then you'll need to think intelligently about what exactly needs to be renamed/recreated, and do some name substitution in these instructions. Hopefully that's clear!

> Unfortunately the most recent installer installs some kind of incompatible vc2005 runtime, messing up the winetricks installation.
After the setup is complete, move the Turbine program folder from the .wine directory to another location (the desktop in my case).

Delete the .wine directory, I don't have any other windows programs installed in my case, others have setup up a specific wine directory for MoM as detailed in previous messages.
Go to Applications->Wine->Configure Wine from the top menubar, this sets up a new .wine directory automatically.
Move the Turbine folder back under the Program Files directory.

From the command prompt:
```
sudo apt-get install cabextract
./winetricks
```

The winetricks command opens up a graphical menu, choose directx9, vcrun2005 and vcrun2005sp1 and then click ok.
Go to applications->games->The lord of the rings online.
Go to Tools->options and switch the game directory to the game directory.
Log in!

If you're using wineprefix, I think you should make sure you first create your wineprefix and make it active (export) and then run your winetricks and do the regedits for that wineprefix. And then try patching. Somebody please correct me if I'm wrong, but I think that's how the wineprexises are supposed to work (I have no need for using it, but vaguely remember reading some help page about it).

PS.   If this still doesn't work: what is your graphics card? Do you have a recent driver installed for it?

Your nvidia driver is not the latest (I'm running 180.44) but it shouldn't cause things like that, I think the 170 version is recent enouhg.

By the way, what did you end up doing to fix the previous problems? Please post about that, it could be useful to somebody else later on.

### Post by martialartist81 on 2009-04-01
Great news!  I'm up and running beautifully!  Thanks again to everyone for their help and guidance!

Below, I typed up what steps I followed from start-to-finish to make the game/launcher work.  **Note:** I am merely piecing together all of the work of many other people.  I just followed what they told me to do and voila!  Working game in Linux.  I take absolutely no credit for these guys' blood, sweat, and coding tears.  I'm merely putting it all down the way that I followed it and got it working.

1. Fresh install of 8.10 Ubuntu.
2. Updated the OS/files.
3. Installed WINE, then added the Ubuntu/Deb repository. Directions [HERE]("http://www.winehq.org/download/deb")
4. In a terminal, verified version of WINE
wine --version

Mine came back as:
wine-1.1.18

5. Open a new terminal (and make sure you remember this one, you'll want to keep it open for awhile).
6. In the new terminal, run the following commands:

export WINEPREFIX=$HOME/.wine-lotro
wineprefixcreate
wget -nv [http://www.kegel.com/wine/winetricks](http://www.kegel.com/wine/winetricks)
chmod 700 ./winetricks
./winetricks gecko
./winetricks vcrun2005
./winetricks vcrun2005sp1

7. Those commands will create a new directory "./wine-lotro" for you to use instead of the standard ./wine directory.  After you run all the winetricks, in the SAME terminal, run:

regedit

This opens up the WINE Registry Edit.
8. From here, click on the + for HKEY_CURRENT_USER/Software/Wine
9. Right click the "Wine" folder and select "New" - "Key"
10. Make the new key "Direct3D"
11. Right click the "Direct3D" and make "New" - "String Value"
12. Label the "String Value" as "VideoMemorySize"
13. "VideoMemorySize" will be the amount of Video Memory that your Graphics Card has (i.e. "256" for a 256MB card, etc).
14. Go to [HERE]("http://turbine.fuzeqna.com/lotro.support/consumer/kbdetail.asp?kbid=1554") and select the "Standard-resolution" version of the download (I haven't tried the hi-res version).
15. Once your download finishes, it should automatically kick off the installation.  At this point, you want to make sure that you install the game to your ./wine-lotro directory and not the ./wine directory.
16. Now that the game is installed, Go to "System - Administration - Software Sources."
17. Select "Third-Party Software" and "Add" the following:

deb [http://ppa.launchpad.net/ajackson-bcs/ppa/ubuntu](http://ppa.launchpad.net/ajackson-bcs/ppa/ubuntu) hardy main

**NOTE:** I'm currently using 8.10 Intrepid, so don't be alarmed at the "hardy main" portion of it.  It's the only thing available and yes, it has to be added just as it reads above.

18. Go to [HERE]("http://www.lotrolinux.com/download.php") and right-click the "key-file" hyperlink and "Save-as" onto your desktop.  Leave it the default name, no need to change it.
19. Back in your Software Sources, select the "Authentication" tab, and click "Import Key File" - Now Select the file that you just saved to your desktop.
20. Click "Close" on your Software Sources window, and you'll be asked to Reload the Sources; do so now.
21. To install the GUI, open up a new terminal and type: 

sudo apt-get install lotrolinux

**Note:** I had issues after adding the Repository and the key-file, and step #21 resolved me not being able to locate the Lotrolinux GUI app.

There's some kind of issue with the newest Lotro Installer (the game itself) that messes with the vc2005 runtime.  So here's what we have to do to fix it before running the launcher:

22. Find your "Turbine" directory, and cut/paste it from it's location to the desktop.
23. Delete your ./wine-lotro directory completely.
24. Move your "Turbine" directory over to your ./wine folder (or... see "Addendum #1" at the bottom of this post if you want to use another ./wine-lotro directory)
25. In a terminal, type:

sudo apt-get install cabextract
./winetricks

26. Winetricks will open up a GUI menu.  Select "directx9, vcrun2005 and vcrun2005sp1" and then click "OK"
27. Now, go to "Applications - Games - The Lord of the Rings Online"
28. Once it opens, go to "Tools - Patch".  Click "Apply" to start the patching process (this can take awhile, so grab a cup of coffee, read an Ubuntu Book, whatever!)
28a. Go to "Applications - Wine - Configure Wine" and make sure to select "Windows 2000" instead of "Windows XP" for your Windows Version.
29. Now that the patching process is completed, log into the game.
29a. One of two things should happen, it should crashing giving you a "UserPreferences.ini" error OR the game will actually load.
29b. Either way, the "UserPreferences.ini" file will be created the first time you launch the game, let's go to that file (./wine/drive_c/windows/profiles/<your username>/My Documents/The Lord of the Rings Online).
30. Open that file, and change the following two values:
AllowFakeFullScreen=False
FullScreen=True

31. Save the file, close it, now start the GUI Launcher again.
32. /DrumRoll...... It should be working!

**_Addendum #1_**
At Step #24, if you wanted to make another ./wine-lotro directory to keep your LotRO game outside of anything else that Wine runs for you, after you delete the .wine-lotro directory, open up a terminal and type:
export WINEPREFIX=$HOME/.wine-lotro
- That will create the /.wine-lotro directory for you again.
- From that SAME terminal, run Steps #25 and #26.
- Continue the remainder of the directions, and you should be good to go.


**_References/Links:_**
"ajackson" created the GUI and provided tremendous help here's his page (which he no longer maintains, but all the links still are up and running and without which, the GUI would not be possible) [HERE]("http://www.lotrolinux.com/")

[LotRO Lorebook Entry for Linux and Mac OSX]("http://lorebook.lotro.com/wiki/LOTRO_under_Linux_and_Mac_OS/X")

[WINE AppDB Entry/Steps]("http://appdb.winehq.org/objectManager.php?sClass=version&iId=14566")

**_Special Thanks:_**
"ajackson" for creating the GUI and all the help
"wylfing" for his help pointing me in the right direction for some of the fixes
"BatPenguin" for his graphical help and pointers.
"SNy" the creator of the CLI launcher (found [HERE]("http://www.bmx-chemnitz.de/~mfr/LOTRO/")) and all his help getting me up to snuff to launch the game
Thanks to anyone and everyone on the Ubuntuforums.org for all their help and guidance!  You guys/gals are amazing!

---

### Post by ajackson on 2009-04-01
> **martialartist81 said:**
> 6. In the new terminal, run the following commands:

export WINEPREFIX=$HOME/.wine-lotro
wineprefixcreate
wget -nv [http://www.kegel.com/wine/winetricks](http://www.kegel.com/wine/winetricks)
chmod 700 ./winetricks
./winetricks gecko
./winetricks vcrun2005
./winetricks vcrun2005sp1
If you are installing from the MoM DVD or download you can safely skip the winetricks part for vcrun2005 & vcrun2005sp1 as the installer takes care of that for you. In fact it seems if you add vcrun yourself and then use the MoM installer you will sometimes (4 out of 5 times I tried it) get the errors about C++ runtime when you try launching/patching.

If you skip those and the game doesn't start you can always try adding them after.

> 15. Once your download finishes, it should automatically kick off the installation.  At this point, you want to make sure that you install the game to your ./wine-lotro directory and not the ./wine directory.
if you ran the installer in the terminal that you exported WINEPREFIX to it will go into that directory, if you aren't certain if the WINEPREFIX is set you can check with ```
echo $WINEPREFIX
```

> There's some kind of issue with the newest Lotro Installer (the game itself) that messes with the vc2005 runtime.  So here's what we have to do to fix it before running the launcher:
See my point at the top, if you are going for a fresh install you don't need to install the vc2005 runtimes, if you are upgrading from a pre-MoM install then you do (though try it first to save any messing around)

### Post by wylfing on 2009-04-04
> **uns3r said:**
> i followed martialartist81 guide and when i got to the launcher what am i supposed to put for the launcher configuration data 
1: parameters
2: WINEDEBUG
3: WINEPREFIX
4:Game Directory

i need to know this information to update and to launch lotro

You can leave Parameters blank. WINEDEBUG can be left at the default value of "fixme-all". If you installed LoTRO in .wine, then you can leave WINEPREFIX blank. The Game Directory is the location of lotroclient.exe.

HTH

### Post by ajackson on 2009-04-05
**PyLotRO 0.1.0**
As hinted to earlier (probably a few pages back by now), I have come out of LotRO retirement and am enjoying the game again (on the US servers now after someone kindly gave me an activation key for both SoA and MoM). Maybe it's just being away from the tender mercies of Codemasters or the game has got better (or I'm not as grumpy :)) but I'm having fun again.

LotROLinux has a few things I don't really like about it, the patching window isn't all that good and I seem to have hit a few problems with Mono and the Gecko browser (might be related to my system usually being the latest test version of Ubuntu). I have also took in one of the main criticisms of LotROLinux, it is fiddly to configure (at times).

So LotROLinux is dead and a new Python based version is in it's place, PyLotRO (yes it didn't take me long to think of the name :)).

At the moment there is only a tarball download ( [www.lotrolinux.com/PyLotRO-0.1.0.tar.gz](www.lotrolinux.com/PyLotRO-0.1.0.tar.gz) ), the debs will be created when I work out how to create a deb from a Python script bundle.

Installing is very easy, extract the tarball, cd into the directory and run
```
sudo ./setup.py install
```
or
```
sudo python setup.py install
```

Since it creates an identical menu entry as LotROLinux it is probably worth uninstalling that first :)

It uses the same config file as LotROLinux as that makes the transition easier. At the moment the changes are fairly cosmetic.

**Patching**
The output from the patching process is piped directly into the log window, I no longer parse it in anyway and there is an option to save the contents of that log window if you wish. Note the same problem LotROLinux had with the patching sometimes hanging still happens, it is outside my control (the fired off process doesn't terminate right so my program doesn't realise it has finished) but the Exit button on the log window terminates the process without causing the main app to get upset.

**Running**
As with patching a log window is displayed which captures everything that is spewed out by running the game, including any error messages you might get it there is a problem starting wine (before the app would just close and you'd have no idea what the problem is).

**Set Up Wizard**
The main new feature. You tell it which game slot you want to fill (two for LotRO and two for DDO) and what you are using to run the game (wine, Crossover Games, Crossover), click the find button and (hopefully) it will show you the WINEPREFIX (or bottle is using CXG/CXO) and the relevant part of the game directory. It works by (for wine) looking for directories starting with a dot (ie .wine-lotro) and seeing if they have a wine set up in them (drive_c/Program Files), if so it scans the Program Files directory & subdirectories for the client file name (lotroclient.exe or dndclient.exe). CXG/CXO has it slightly easier as they have a standard path for storing the bottles.

It then fills out a table, you click the one you want and it configures the launcher for you. If it finds the high-res graphics file it assumes you want high-res enabled and it assumes that you are using patchclient.dll for patching (only DDO needs to change this). If you need to change either the options window is pretty much identical to LotROLinux.


At present I can only really confirm that it works OK with Linux, I've got to set my Mac OS X set up back up to get it tested using that. Also from a Mac OS X point of view version 0.2.0 will hopefully deal with starting up CXG/CXO when needed so that bundling it in an app file is a lot easier.

**What do you need?**
You need python (installed by default on most version of Linux), I've coded it using 2.6 but it should work fine with 2.5.

PyQT4 - Moved away from GTK because of a lack of decent in-built web browser component. As far as I can tell Gutsy was the first Ubuntu release to contain PyQT4, prior to Intrepid the web browser was not included but the launcher automatically switches to a text version if it can't create the html version. The package you need is python-qt4, it will probably install a few other packages when you install that but python-qt4 is the one you need.

4Suite - this does the bulk of the XML manipulation (the config files are all XML) and handles the generation of the news page. The package is python-4suite-xml.

**The future**
As I mentioned the goal of the next point release (0.2.0) is getting the Mac side running right.

One addition I have considered is adding an option to make installing the stand alone patches easier (since it normally is best to install LotRO/DDO in it's own bottle).

Another thing I'm considering is getting the launcher to help with the initial install of the game, since I know WINEPREFIXs do confuse some people (luckily CXG/CXO already have a nice gui so I only have to worry about people using wine).

The last nice to have would be an (optional) patch reminder, maybe set up something that knows when scheduled maintenance days are, knows when you last patched and prompts you to run the patcher after a set period or the first time after normal maintenance.


So any feedback on those 3 options or PyLotRO in general (especially the set up wizard) is appreciated.

**Important Note:** When you created a forum ID on these forums you agree to the forums code of conducts, one of those codes mentions not PMing people for support. I have had a lot of PMs asking for support - I will ignore them if I get them, get me on a bad day and I might well ignore any subsequent requests for help via the forums as well (as someone is currently finding out :evil: ).

It's not a great deal to ask.

---

### Post by ajackson on 2009-04-05
> **BatPenguin said:**
> And thanks again for working on the launcher, it's great that you want to improve Lotrolinux (although I find the GUI launcher to be great even in its current form).
It might just be issues with Jaunty but it seems to take an age authenticating and getting the initial configuration settings off the server.

Part of the reasoning was that the Mac port suffered, so I knew if I ever started up again something had to change, using Python and Qt4 makes it easier to maintain one version rather than one and a badly hacked half :)

> Anyway, I just downloaded the python launcher, installed it and tried to give it a go, but got hit by this:

```
xml.sax._exceptions.SAXParseException: file:///usr/share/xml/gnustep/gsdoc-1_0_0.dtd:41:0: syntax error

```
Seems it is a known bug conflicting with the 4suite libraries ( [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gnustep-base/+bug/301253](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gnustep-base/+bug/301253) ).

I just tried it with gnustep-base-common installed and get the same error, so until it's fixed you can either try the workaround shown in the bug report or remove gnustep-base-common. Neither is ideal as presumable the entries that are causing the problem are needed for something.

> EDIT: Would be nice to have a clear uninstall option!
Yeah, the python install method doesn't (yet) come with one but I'll knock up something and include it.

### Post by ajackson on 2009-04-06
My favourite sort of bug, easy to fix.

**PyLotRO 0.1.1**
Fixed the problem under Hardy with it not return the path correctly from the set up wizard, and corrected the issues causing the QVariant error messages.

Added an option to setup.py to allow for installation
```
sudo ./setup.py uninstall
```
**NB:** As long as you installed using 0.1.1s set up then you can uninstall it later as it passes an extra parameter to capture a log (pylotro-install.log) of the install process and uses that file to remove the installed files when ran with the uninstall option.

Tarball is ( [http://www.lotrolinux.com/PyLotRO-0.1.1.tar.bz2](http://www.lotrolinux.com/PyLotRO-0.1.1.tar.bz2) ), also updated the downloads page on [http://www.lotrolinux.com](http://www.lotrolinux.com) to contain information about PyLotRO and a link to the latest tarball.

---

Thanks again!  Great stuff and keep up the tremendous work!

### Post by BatPenguin on 2009-04-08
> **uns3r said:**
> what does he mean by cd into the directory

I don't think anybody is trying to be rude on purpose by not answering your question, but it seems like you should get more familiar with the command line and the layout of a Linux computer (or perhaps any OS) before you'll have a good chance of getting Lotro to run in Ubuntu. This is, really, not a very easy thing to do to get the game to run and most of the work has to be done on the command line, not the GUI. Your question demonstrates that you should first read a few HOW-TOs on using the command line, moving between directories, copying files, changing permissions and such. These forums probably have such how-to's, take a look around, study them for an evening and get to know your computer better. Then see if the instructions make more sense, and ask specific questions - politely - again if you have problems. When asking questions, start by describing everything you've done in detail and what how-to's you've followed and such.

Alright, about your question: "Cd into the directory" refers to the command "cd" (change directory). "cd /home/uns3r" will change the currect directory to "/home/uns3r". This is one of the most basic command line commands. Really, start by reading up on how-to's about how your computers directories and files are organized and how to move about the file system and manipulate it.

---

### Post by ajackson on 2009-04-08
**PyLotRO 0.1.2**
Couple of changes, one to reduce of stuff spewed out if a server responds with a blank (the current error trapping gets it and reports it but if you were running via a terminal you would have seen some garbage).

The second change is more being friendly towards people who download the source tarball onto a non case sensitive file system (*cough* windows *cough*), the PyLotRO folder has been renamed PyLotROLauncher.

This could leave some junk behind, there are two ways of removing it, use the 0.1.1 tarball to uninstall (if you have a pylotro-install.log, if not install using 0.1.1 then uninstall).

Or look in /usr/local/lib/python2.5/site-packages (or python2.6/dist-packages) and remove the PyLotRO directory. It doesn't cause any harm being there but why waste disk space.

Debs are now available for Hardy, Intrepid & Jaunty (see the link in my signature, then head to the download page for more info).

The source code is being housed on launchpad rather than sourceforge but I'm still setting it up. It will include a bug tracker but for bugs with PyLotRO, not LotRO or DDO (or questions about how to configure either the launcher or the games)

---

### Post by wylfing on 2009-04-09
The main "bother" that made me want an uninstaller for PyLotRO was the .desktop file. If you decide you don't like PyLotRO and want to go back to the old lotrolinux launcher, you'll end up with two identical-looking entries under Applications > Games. Mere mortals should not have to enter /usr/share manually, they might never make it back out alive. That task is best left to machines (i.e., package managers :p).

Anyway, I'd guess the debs take care of that now. Nice work!

### Post by BatPenguin on 2009-04-10
> **ajackson said:**
> I just tried it with gnustep-base-common installed and get the same error, so until it's fixed you can either try the workaround shown in the bug report or remove gnustep-base-common.

Alright, I just tried the new pylotro again with  gnustep-base-common removed: looks great! The directory auto-detect seemed to work fine for me, no problems. Nice to get more info on patches (although there wasn't anything to download, but the window looked like it would show more). I guess I notice a little speed difference too, this one seems to get the server info faster, as you said. And the window graphics are slightly snappier looking somehow...can't put my finger on it, but even my wife said "it looks nicer now" so must be true :) So thanks, great work!

You asked for input about features, so this is just wishful thinking now, feel free to shoot it down (hey, you asked! heh):

Anyway, you mentioned installation help. I think that's a wonderful idea if you could somehow incorporate a game installer function into the program. We seem to get installation problem messages here constantly from people of various Linux/Wine skills. So the best would of course be if the installer did something like: 

- see if the user is running the restricted Nvidia driver, prompt to install if not  
- create another wine prefix for Lotro
- set the settings for it (probe if possible, but prompting "how much graphics memory you have" or something would be great too)
- download and install winetricks if necessary 
- ask the user to insert the DVD or point to an install file

etc. don't know if there were other things required, been so long since I last installed that I just don't even remember how to exactly install this, hehe.

Anyway, if something like that was possibe, I'm sure the crowds would be toasting your glory at the Prancing Pony in no time :)

Even a simpler "Thank you for installing the Linux launcher for Lord of the Rings Online, now please do the following:" type of how-to _inside_ the launcher itself with or without a method or detecting whether the installation stage has been completed would probably be really useful, just a list where you tick off a box and it shows you the next stage. It seems like many of these questions we get (even the polite, nicely worded ones :p pretty frequently have to do with quite basic running of scripts or checking permissions, making sure somebody hasn't run anything as sudo etc. so installation help inside the launcher would probably go a long way with helping people.

Anyway, just my few eurocents. Thanks again! Great work.

### Post by ajackson on 2009-04-10
> **lonegunman said:**
> I decided on the non-mozilla gui installer
and now I get no errors it just doesnt run after i log in. The process just disappears after a bit.
Can you try again with PyLotRO, that catches everything that is displayed when running so might show what is causing it to die.

Could you also post your wine version number (type wine --version in a terminal window) and what graphics card/drivers

> Or if I try running directly from wine it says "you must first install one of the following versions of .NET" 
The games native launcher doesn't run under wine, it might be that the stuff above was caused by running this one. My launcher sticks a menu option in the games sub menu but not on the desktop. Double check that you are running the right launcher (if all else fails run type LotROLinux or pylotro in a terminal window).

> So then I tried the command line import of the fake file and I got
"err:process:__wine_kernel_init boot event wait timed out"
That does sound like an odd error message, try sticking that in a google search as I've never seen an error like that before.

As a note, the fake registry entry would just stop .NET 1.1 from being installed if you did it prior to running the installer. At the time that hack was created .NET 1.1 caused the installer to crash but nowadays it isn't really needed (you may get an error message during install but the game does install)

---

### Post by ajackson on 2009-04-10
> **BatPenguin said:**
> Anyway, you mentioned installation help. I think that's a wonderful idea if you could somehow incorporate a game installer function into the program. We seem to get installation problem messages here constantly from people of various Linux/Wine skills. So the best would of course be if the installer did something like:
I like the idea, don't know how easy it would be considering different linux systems install things in different ways (not to mention OS X). 

> - see if the user is running the restricted Nvidia driver, prompt to install if not
While I wouldn't be able to install them I could at least check if direct rendering is set to yes and tell them to check graphics drivers if not (with a heavy hint to check their distros web site for more info)

> - create another wine prefix for Lotro
- set the settings for it (probe if possible, but prompting "how much graphics memory you have" or something would be great too)
The creation and configuration of the prefix is a very good idea, people do struggle with the concept of creating the prefix. CXG has made it easy to do from their point but Wine still could do with something to help (and make sure that it is set to windows 2000, the number of times that is skipped over is amazing).

> - download and install winetricks if necessary
I think that I've worked out how to detect if the C++ run-times are installed, I also now know how to stop it installing .NET 1.1 and 2.0 (it seems if you install the winetricks version of the run-times when the other one and .NET 2.0 are installed, that is what caused those MScoreworks errors or whatever the module name is)

> - ask the user to insert the DVD or point to an install file
That leads on from my idea for letting them install the standalone patches using the launcher, the same process can be used for installing the game in the first place.

> Anyway, just my few eurocents. Thanks again! Great work.
Good ideas, something to think about.

---

### Post by wylfing on 2009-04-10
PyLotRO feedback -

Nice work on the deb, sir! Works great. Uninstalled lotrolinux for me just like it should.

Also, I second that it looks much nicer.

Just for kicks, I did Tools > Patch and clicked Start. This proceeded to crash. In the wine output window it displayed the following:
```
fixme:mixer:ALSA_MixerInit No master control found on USB Device 0x46d:0x9a4, disabling mixer

Unrecognized argument 'HOME/BRIAN/.WINE/DRIVE_C/PROGRAM'

                    : <patch server>: The address:port of the 
                      patch server.
    --app           : The application directory. (default is '.')
    --backup        : The backup directory. (default is '.\backup')
    --dataonly      : only download and patch data.
    --filesonly     : only download and patch files.
    --forward       : The forward directory. (default is '.\forward')
    --highres       : Patch high res data.
    --language      : The language to patch.
    --noapply       : download patches but don't apply them.
    --productcode   : Unused. Provided here for backward compatibility.
    --temp          : The temp directory. (default is '.\temp')

GetConsoleScreenBufferInfo 5
```
The first bit (Device 0x46d:0x9a4) is just wine having fits about my Logitech Messenger webcam (which nevertheless works fine, go figure). but the second thing is the issue -- it looks like the space between Program and Files is causing things to break. The ALL CAPS could be an issue too, but I suspect the space first.

EDIT: I then tried to run the game and it also crashed. There was a wine-generated error dialog that popped up, which said "The game client was passed invalid command-line parameters. [205]". In the wine output window was this (in addition to a long 'help' list of legal parameters):
```
Argument 'ONLINE/CLIENT_LOCAL_ENGLISH' unknown
```

### Post by kielcary on 2009-04-10
Hi there guys -

I spent about 4 hours last night trying to get things working, and I finally succeeded to a certain extent.

PyLotro is working well for me with the latest updates from Ubuntu and Wine (set in Win 2000 emulation mode).

At first I had just a couple FPS and it wasn't playable.  However, I went to AMD's website and got the newest ATI drivers (released 3/26) and suddenly it became playable.

My frame rate still isn't very good, but it's playable on low settings and medium resolution.  Turning off Compiz and my dock don't really seem to be doing much for the frame rate.

I've been browsing through these forums looking at different settings to change and whatnot.  I've made the two .ini changes and added the HKEY for memory allocation using regedit.  Are there any other tweaks you can recommend to help out with things?  I'd like to get it running a bit more smoothly than it is.

I'm a developer, so LOTRO was my last holdover on Windows.  I don't play many games, and that was about it for things that Wine or Cedega didn't install without any hassle.  Now that it runs, I can get rid of my Windows partition and move on with life :)

Anyway, thanks for all of your help and, again, please let me know any more tweaks you can think of!

### Post by ajackson on 2009-04-10
> **wylfing said:**
> Just for kicks, I did Tools > Patch and clicked Start. This proceeded to crash. In the wine output window it displayed the following:
```
fixme:mixer:ALSA_MixerInit No master control found on USB Device 0x46d:0x9a4, disabling mixer

Unrecognized argument 'HOME/BRIAN/.WINE/DRIVE_C/PROGRAM'
```
Sound like a configuration error as it seems to be passing a strange value (the Program Files, etc bit). Could you note down what the various entries in the settings windows are.

> EDIT: I then tried to run the game and it also crashed. There was a wine-generated error dialog that popped up, which said "The game client was passed invalid command-line parameters. [205]". In the wine output window was this (in addition to a long 'help' list of legal parameters):
```
Argument 'ONLINE/CLIENT_LOCAL_ENGLISH' unknown
```
That does suggest it is something to do with it detecting the language file wrong. In your LotRO directory there should be a file called client_local_xxx.dat, where xxx is probably English in your case. What is it's exact filename (including case as I think that is the problem)?

---

### Post by ajackson on 2009-04-10
> **kielcary said:**
> Are there any other tweaks you can recommend to help out with things?
The obvious option is turning the post processing off (in advanced graphics). In the early days of getting LotRO and Wine working that caused a lot of graphical glitches.

It is more or less trial and error, look for the options where the tooltip mentions performance.

Since you have an ATI card I can't offer too many specific settings as I only know how certain ones tend to affect my aging NVidia card, I think a couple of others who follow this thread use ATI cards, they might be able to offer better advice.

Why?!

EDIT. OK re-installing Wine, redoing it all and we'll see what happens.

EDIT2: Now everything works. This is basically what I had to do;

1. Completely uninstall wine, clear out all wine or lotro-wine related folders.
2. Reinstalled wine. Then changed to windows 2000 mode in Configure Wine from the menu.
3. Edited my registry to add the memorysize key
4. Grabbed winetricks
5. Ran winetricks and enabled/installed directx9, vcrun2005, and vcrun2005sp1
6. Grabbed and installed pylotro from the repository.
7. Put my Turbine/The Lord of the Rings folder from a previous fulled patch install in my program files in the .wine folder
8. ran pylotro 
4. set my game path in tools/settings wizard
5. Ran the game :)

1. put sata hd in compatibility mode

### Post by ajackson on 2009-04-12
> **lonegunman said:**
> 14. ran bash winetricks vcrun2005 from same terminal, clicked yes
15. ran bash winetricks vcrun2005sp1 from same terminal, clicked yes, exited terminal
16. downloaded the hi-res installer from [http://turbine.fuzeqna.com/lotro.support/consumer/kbdetail.asp?kbid=1554](http://turbine.fuzeqna.com/lotro.support/consumer/kbdetail.asp?kbid=1554)

If you are using the MoM installer you do not need to install anything via winetricks, the installer will set up the C++ run-time for you. In fact installing the C++ run-time then using the installer (or using the installer then adding the run-time) seems to be what causes the mscorsvw.exe problem.

### Post by wylfing on 2009-04-15
> **Devilman13 said:**
> Just as a side note, pyLOTRO is running well in Wine 1.1.19... if not better :)

Pylotro itself doesn't run in wine, it runs in...well, Python. But the point is still valid. Wine 1.1.19 works well for me too, although the black icons continue to haunt me.

There's a ton of misconception about how to get LotRO installed, mainly I think due to the wine appdb page, which lists a whole lot of steps that are completely unnecessary. At this point, it pretty much goes like this:

STEP 1: Download the installer from the LotRO web site and run it.
STEP 2: Install pylotro.
STEP 3: Play the game.

(Obviously you'll do some game patching in there too, but that's a normal part of playing online games.) Doing all those extra steps is what gets people in trouble. At the very end, if the game won't launch, you might need to do the move-it-away, winetricks, move-it-back gig. That's really about it, though.

### Post by wylfing on 2009-04-17
> **Devilman13 said:**
> pyLOTRO ran so slow that even watching the swoosh of the turbine logo was UNBEARABLE!

By the time the actual game is up and running, pylotro has already done its thing (i.e., launch the game) and exited. You're in 100% wine country in-game. So if you're having trouble with slow performance we need to look at other causes -- video driver probably being the first suspect. Post back with how the re-install went and if you're still having performance trouble we'll try to get it sorted out.

Just be sure to keep the install as simple as possible. Download the MoM installer from lotro.com. Don't use the one on the DVD. Only do "extra" steps when something isn't working.

> So, tonight I'll be going about clearing all my Wine stuff out and starting all over :cry:

I feel your pain. I probably installed LotRO more than a dozen times before i did it right and got a working game.

---

**Weekend Update (working now)**:

- I removed Wine, and deleted its folders, then did a "locate wine" to find and remove some shortcuts and crap I didn't want laying around

- After a few tries the way I **wanted** to do it, I ended up just trying to go back to my original setup to just get the game running properly again. I also re-installed the newest nvidia update as I noticed this time around it was making other games perform better.

- Re-installed wine. Added the registry key for the video size with regedit. Switched wine to win2000 mode. Downloaded winetricks and just ran winetricks with ./winetricks instead of specifying different elements through the command line. I selected the following things:

- Directx9, Microsoft .net 1.1, vcrun2005, and vcrun2005sp1

- For some reason installing from the installer from the Internet kept giving me weird errors saying something to extent of either my client was missing or completely screwed up (??) I ended up copying the Turbine folder from my girlfriends computer that installed the game with Windows XP from the DVDs.

- I used AJacksons original LOTRO-Linux program I'm sad to say... not pyLOTRO. My reason for that being it "just works" for me, and I don't really need the fanciness of the python version. 

- I played the game. The first thing I noticed is that instantly the game wanted to detect higher settings than I remember it doing on my last couple of attempts... hmm.

### Post by ajackson on 2009-04-20
**PyLotRO 0.1.3**
Four changes:

_Fixed error in AuthenticateUser_
You would only notice it if running via a terminal (spewed out a bit of garbage)

_Added make_app to setup.py to allow creation of MacOSX .app file_
This just the first draft of creating the directory structure and putting the right files in the right places (hopefully), the internal launcher changes haven't been written you so I doubt if it would work

_Added configuration checker_
Shows the Windows version, amount of graphics memory and if VC++ Run 2005 is installed. I'll be adding options to correct the winver and memory and install VC++ Run 2005 (LotRO only as I don't think DDO needs it)

_Convert code to be compatible with Py3.0 as well as Py2.x_
The supporting libraries (PyQT4, 4Suite) aren't compatible yet and no doubt some function calls will change when they do update.

Source on my site (see sig), DEBs being built.

---

### Post by ajackson on 2009-04-20
> **Devilman13 said:**
> - I used AJacksons original LOTRO-Linux program I'm sad to say... not pyLOTRO. My reason for that being it "just works" for me, and I don't really need the fanciness of the python version. 
Don't be sad to say that, if you like that one and it works for you use it. I no longer "officially" support it but the internals of how that and PyLotRO work are virtually identical so trouble shooting is pretty easy.

### Post by ajackson on 2009-04-24
**PyLotRO 0.1.4**
Couple of bug fixes plus a bit more

Log windows (Patch/Run Game) exit button renamed abort while running - display message when finished/aborted

Finished MacOSX .app file functions (still in testing, when confirmed OK version will jump to 0.2.0)

DDO test server uses login queues [365538]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/pylotro/+bug/365538")

Some versions of PyQt4 had problem with multi-line message in ui/winSelectAccount.ui [365541]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/pylotro/+bug/365541")

I know it doesn't help when people say this, but just to let you know it's possible - I keep compiz on and don't experience any problems.

### Post by ajackson on 2009-04-25
> **wylfing said:**
> Oddly enough, my Hardy desktop plays sound 100% but my wife's Intrepid desktop won't play sound in videos.
Pulseaudio could cause that. I have to kill pulseaudio before starting LotRO or it will occasionally crash when in alsa mode.

Pulseaudio also comes with a (usually) helpful feature that makes it restart if it crashes, of course you kill it and it thinks it's crashed (look in /etc/pulse/client.conf look for autospawn and set to no to stop that behaviour).

> I know it doesn't help when people say this, but just to let you know it's possible - I keep compiz on and don't experience any problems.

What I do for "windowed mode" is make wine simulate a desktop with the dimensions I want from a "window" and then let the game think it's running full screen.
I think it varies for different graphics cards/drivers/etc

---

### Post by ajackson on 2009-04-25
> **Devilman13 said:**
> I tried pyLOTRO again, but it is still causing me severe performance issues. It starts up nice and everything thing seems to be ok.. but then I notice things getting more and more laggy over the period of about five minutes to the point where the sound is popping off and on and my character's animations are like a slide show.
I've just release 0.1.5, this release should stop pylotro gobbling up CPU cycles when it is doing very little.

If you are using alsa and have pulseaudio loaded that can cause things to go into slideshow mode (as it crashes and then restarts, crashes, restarts, etc)

The source is here, I don't have any of the debs as they were never on my computer.

### Post by ajackson on 2009-04-27
> **Bunzinator said:**
> I've set HKEY_CURRENT_USER/Software/Wine/Direct3D/VideoMemorySize to 2048. (probably more than I need, but hey.)
Unless you have a graphics card with 2GB of memory (which I doubt) there is little wonder why it is crashing.

> After running smoothly for 5 minutes or so on high graphics, it crashes with the following wine messages. Looks like it might be PulseAudio trying to allocate.
If Wine is using Alsa (I think it does by default) it is usually best to kill pulseaudio before using wine, the alternative is to set wine to use oss but then you don't get sound in the cut scenes.

### Post by lonegunman on 2009-04-28
> **Lissi said:**
> Hello,

First of all I'd like to say I'm very new to Linux. :)

I've followed the step by step article in the "LOTRO under Linux and Mac OS/X" lorebook as well as the install instructions in the readme for the GUI Launcher however I just can't get the launcher to run.  Everytime I click it nothing happens.  The lorebook article says to install Mono and as far as I can tell it is installed.  Is there anything else I may have missed?  Perhaps it's me being silly but I've been searching for a soloution for this for three days now.  I'd really love to be able to get back into Middle Earth without having to install Windows just to run the game.

Thank you in advance for your time and help :)
Lissi

Hi Lissi,

First off I wouldn't follow that website. 
1. Download the Mines of Moria standard res installer from (I have yet to be successful with the high res installer but I can get the standard to work on my ati and my nvidia card, note you can't run at ultra high graphics mode with the standard installer but very high and below are fine.)
[http://turbine.fuzeqna.com/lotro.support/consumer/kbdetail.asp?kbid=1554](http://turbine.fuzeqna.com/lotro.support/consumer/kbdetail.asp?kbid=1554)
2. Add wine and pylotro lastest versions by adding them to your repository and keys from here
[http://www.winehq.org/download/deb](http://www.winehq.org/download/deb)
[http://www.lotrolinux.com/download.php](http://www.lotrolinux.com/download.php)
3. Install your latest graphics driver, pylotro, & wine.
5. Run wine config (applications -> wine -> configure wine) and set up your audio (click apply when done) and set the default to windows 2000 (on the applications tab and apply when done)
4. Install mine of moria using wine (may take a very long time) DO NOT run it from wine at the end. Uncheck all the boxes
5. Run pylotro (under applications-> games) select tools and run settings wizard. Cick find games, click on your game then click apply and close pylotro
6. Run pylotro and select tools and then patch followed by start (wait along time again) 
7. Exit the patcher and select your server and login.
A. Make sure if your running full screen to have compiz turned off (system->pref->appearance. go to the visual effects tab and select none)
B. If you want compiz on you can run lotro in windowed mode. (go to applications -> wine -> configure wine. select graphics tab and check the box for emulate virtual desktop and type in the size you want.)
C. If it runs choppy or crashes when you try to go above the low graphics setting you need to add your video memory to the registry (applications->wine->browse C drive. then windows and double click regedit.exe) Once in the registry browse down to HKey_Current_User -> Software -> Wine. Right Click and select new-> Key. Name this key "Direct3D" without the quotes now click on this new key or folder. You will see only default right click and select new->string. Name this "VideoMemorySize" again without the quotes. Now right click on your new string and select modify. Type in the amount of memory your video card has in megabytes. Click ok then exit the registry.

> **Devilman13 said:**
>  I'm really strongly thinking that my random crash issue may be related to sound driver conflicts. 



I hear yah! My laptop headphone jack stoped working with 9.04... works great in 8.10 and in windows. I think the sound driver in 9.04 doesn't jive with my laptop sound card.

> **ajackson said:**
> Unless you have a graphics card with 2GB of memory (which I doubt) there is little wonder why it is crashing.

IIRC its like 1.5 or 1.75 gb of memory on that card. I would say two things... Make sure you put the exact amount in MB and #2 there is the possibility with a card that new that the drivers suck. My 9800 mobile just got support end of may and 9.04 I'm noticing about 50% of the time when I move in or out of an instance it crashes. I didn't have this problem with the 180 driver on 8.10.

---

### Post by ajackson on 2009-04-29
> **Devilman13 said:**
> However... that could have been that I never even got pulseaudio killed properly in 8.10! I didn't do anything special, just issued a pulseaudio --kill and when the swoosh sound occured I thought all was well. I must fess up to my pulseaudio noobness... I didn't have to deal with it a couple of years back running Linux.

Look in /etc/pulse/client.conf look for autospawn and set to no to stop it restarting itself when you kill it. Pulse has changed a fair bit between 8.10 and 9.04, I think before it used to die and Wine the happily used alsa, now it dies and restarts and wine gets confused. I'm not 100% certain on that but it does now respawn in 9.04 unless you tell it not to.

---

Thank you for confirming my suspicions, AJackson.

7. Exit the patcher and select your server and login.
A. Make sure if your running full screen to have compiz turned off (system->pref->appearance. go to the visual effects tab and select none)
B. If you want compiz on you can run lotro in windowed mode. (go to applications -> wine -> configure wine. select graphics tab and check the box for emulate virtual desktop and type in the size you want.)
C. If it runs choppy or crashes when you try to go above the low graphics setting you need to add your video memory to the registry (applications->wine->browse C drive. then windows and double click regedit.exe) Once in the registry browse down to HKey_Current_User -> Software -> Wine. Right Click and select new-> Key. Name this key "Direct3D" without the quotes now click on this new key or folder. You will see only default right click and select new->string. Name this "VideoMemorySize" again without the quotes. Now right click on your new string and select modify. Type in the amount of memory your video card has in megabytes. Click ok then exit the registry.


Thanks a lot for this post.  After a series of failed attempts to even get the game to launch (especially after following the old instructions), I was able to get LOTRO running for the first time on a new Ubuntu 9.04 64 bit install.  I rebooted the system a few times after getting Wine warnings and everything went  perfect when I was finally ready to launch the game.  It runs great on my Core 2 Quad + 8800GT (180.51 driver).  I am using a X-Fi sound card and had no problem with the ALSA sound output.

### Post by ocellaris on 2009-04-30
> **BatPenguin said:**
> This might be quite off-topic, but i'd be very interested in hearing about your sound card. It's a PCI-express card, right? I'm looking for a PCI-express card that'd "just work" in Ubuntu, and I thought the Creative X-Fi had one of the worst support, just a buggy binary driver..am I mistaken? All I can find now are pretty old links to the driver, what did you do to get ALSA sound, did you just kill pulse or did Pulse even work for you in Jaunty? Does Pulse work otherwise? Feel free to PM me as well, if you feel this is taking the conversation too much off-topic, but I'm very curious about that card. Thanks.

I have an older XtremeMusic PCI version of the card and not a newer PCI Express card.  They all use the same Linux driver however so I would think they work the same.  You can find the driver here:

[http://support.creative.com/Products/ProductDetails.aspx?catID=209&CatName=X-Fi&subCatID=208&subCatName=X-Fi&prodID=14066&prodName=Sound+Blaster+X-Fi+XtremeMusic](http://support.creative.com/Products/ProductDetails.aspx?catID=209&CatName=X-Fi&subCatID=208&subCatName=X-Fi&prodID=14066&prodName=Sound+Blaster+X-Fi+XtremeMusic)

The driver is from November 6th, 2008 and supports ALSA PCM Playback, ALSA Record, and ALSA Mixer.  Installing the driver was easy and everything in Linux worked on the first try, to get sound in LOTRO all I did was open Wine Audio Configutation, it defaulted to ALSA, and I clicked Apply.  I have not made any attempt to kill PulseAudio or anything like that and had no audio problems in 4 hours of playing LOTRO.  

Compared to other cards I could definitely be missing something, however I was never one to dig into audio driver feature sets and I was happy this ended up not being something that held me back.

### Post by Silvercloaks on 2009-05-08
> **lonegunman said:**
> Hi Lissi,

First off I wouldn't follow that website. 
1. Download the Mines of Moria standard res installer from (I have yet to be successful with the high res installer but I can get the standard to work on my ati and my nvidia card, note you can't run at ultra high graphics mode with the standard installer but very high and below are fine.)
[http://turbine.fuzeqna.com/lotro.support/consumer/kbdetail.asp?kbid=1554](http://turbine.fuzeqna.com/lotro.support/consumer/kbdetail.asp?kbid=1554)
2. Add wine and pylotro lastest versions by adding them to your repository and keys from here
[http://www.winehq.org/download/deb](http://www.winehq.org/download/deb)
[http://www.lotrolinux.com/download.php](http://www.lotrolinux.com/download.php)
3. Install your latest graphics driver, pylotro, & wine.
5. Run wine config (applications -> wine -> configure wine) and set up your audio (click apply when done) and set the default to windows 2000 (on the applications tab and apply when done)
4. Install mine of moria using wine (may take a very long time) DO NOT run it from wine at the end. Uncheck all the boxes
5. Run pylotro (under applications-> games) select tools and run settings wizard. Cick find games, click on your game then click apply and close pylotro
6. Run pylotro and select tools and then patch followed by start (wait along time again) 
7. Exit the patcher and select your server and login.
A. Make sure if your running full screen to have compiz turned off (system->pref->appearance. go to the visual effects tab and select none)
B. If you want compiz on you can run lotro in windowed mode. (go to applications -> wine -> configure wine. select graphics tab and check the box for emulate virtual desktop and type in the size you want.)
C. If it runs choppy or crashes when you try to go above the low graphics setting you need to add your video memory to the registry (applications->wine->browse C drive. then windows and double click regedit.exe) Once in the registry browse down to HKey_Current_User -> Software -> Wine. Right Click and select new-> Key. Name this key "Direct3D" without the quotes now click on this new key or folder. You will see only default right click and select new->string. Name this "VideoMemorySize" again without the quotes. Now right click on your new string and select modify. Type in the amount of memory your video card has in megabytes. Click ok then exit the registry.

### Post by ajackson on 2009-05-11
**PyLotRO 0.1.7**
A few updates, mainly affecting the stand-alone version of pylotro
- Fixed problem that was stopping Windows version use proper web browser widget
- Windows version now smaller in size (due to allowing py2exe to put all pyc files in zip file)
- Due to issues with child windows drawing under the parent window on some systems, add option under tools so that all child windows hide the parent and then redisplay the parent when finished
- Due to the windows version providing a standalone solution the generation of a Mac OS X app file has been withdrawn

Source and stand-alone on my site, DEBs being built.

### Post by bentron on 2009-05-15
I'm having a problem with LOTRO registering unmodified mouse clicks.  Right after launching the game everything functions normally and as expected, but after some time (I've yet to notice anything that directly causes it) the game no longer registers unmodified mouse clicks on objects in the world (ie, shift+right click to auto loot a corpse/resource node works, but plain right click to loot or left click to target do not).

For the most part, it doesn't affect the UI at all.  I can still drag things in and out of inventory, ctrl+click to open the dressing room, right click to use from inventory or buy from vendor.  I'm not able to right click certain UI elements to pull up a menu though (chat tabs, my portrait to leave a fellowship, companion portrait to release, etc).

In addition, I've found that I'm able to target NPCs and players if I left click their floating name (assuming floating names are displayed).  Given that, I'm inclined to think it has something to do with rendering rather than mouse input itself.

The game is completely playable (with good performance even) as it is, but it would make things so much easier if I could click to target mobs (especially when groupped) rather than having to cycle through with tab or click on the right floating text in a jumble of different targets.

I have a dual monitor setup and run the game in a virtual desktop of 1920x1200. The game itself runs fullscreen at the same resolution.  

I've also tried running the game in a separate X session by itself (no window manager at all) using a separate xorg.conf so that only one monitor is used.  The problem still crops up (in it's own X session) whether wine is set to run in a virtual desktop or not.

I've had this problem since I started playing the game about a week ago and have searched this thread, googled various phrases, and read through the winehq bugs.  Apparently no one else has reported a problem remotely like this, but I was wondering if anyone has any ideas on what may be causing it and/or how to fix it.

If there's any other info I can provide that might be useful, just let me know.

### Post by Devilman13 on 2009-05-21
Hey guys! I've got a weird issue I wanted to run by you all. Recently I downloaded the Wine 1.1.21 source and compiled it with a D3D patch so that I could play Fallout 3. I'd been playing LOTRO also this past week, mostly in desolate areas. 

Well, last night, I warped back to my house in The Shire and saw an extreme horror. The shire had been flooded! LOL of course not literally.. it was like the surface reflections from nearby creeks made the land all around me mirror, if you get what I mean. Turning off surface reflections fixes the problem, but obviously I love the pretty water and it my older Wine setup handled this area fine.

So, I suspect this MAY be caused by a few registry entries I made, not this version of Wine itself. I did not have this issue with Wine 1.1.20 either. Have a look at the registry settings I've made, and perhaps tell me if one of these could be causing the chaos with water reflections:

```
"DirectDrawRenderer"="gdi"
"OffscreenRenderingMode"="fbo"
"PixelShaderMode"="enabled"
"SoftwareEmulation"="enabled"
"UseGLSL"="enable"
"VertexShaderMode"="hardware"
"VideoDescription"="NVIDIA GeForce 9600 GT"
"VideoDriver"="nv4_disp.dll" 
"VideoMemorySize"="512"
```

Note the last 3 lines are just describing my video hardware so those can be ruled out. In the end though, I'm basically trying to run Fallout 3 and LOTRO off the same Wine install. If I have to change 1 or 2 lines of the registry to switch, that's no big deal to me at all. However, if one of you guys has a better idea to work around this, I'm all ears.

---

### Post by ajackson on 2009-05-21
> **Devilman13 said:**
> Note the last 3 lines are just describing my video hardware so those can be ruled out. In the end though, I'm basically trying to run Fallout 3 and LOTRO off the same Wine install. If I have to change 1 or 2 lines of the registry to switch, that's no big deal to me at all. However, if one of you guys has a better idea to work around this, I'm all ears.

That is why I always advise keeping LotRO in a separate prefix/bottle, it seems to be ever so slightly temperamental. I couldn't even take a wild guess at which one or ones are the cause but luckily once you create a new prefix/bottle you can just move your LotRO install over without needing to reinstall it.

---

### Post by BatPenguin on 2009-05-21
> **Devilman13 said:**
> 

Note the last 3 lines are just describing my video hardware so those can be ruled out. In the end though, I'm basically trying to run Fallout 3 and LOTRO off the same Wine install. If I have to change 1 or 2 lines of the registry to switch, that's no big deal to me at all. However, if one of you guys has a better idea to work around this, I'm all ears.

Ajackson already said it, but I'll just second it: I play Fallout3 as well, and I can't run it under the same wine install as Lotro either. For me the problem was Fallout3 not working (even though I was using the patched wine executable/tree for running it), Lotro has always ran just fine (well, hi-textures crash it but still). But when I just made a separate prefix for fallout and ran that with it, everything's been just fine. So I suggest you just install FO3 into another prefix, keep the patched wine separate from the normal (Lotro) wine under this new prefix, and make a launcher for Fallout for youself with the prefix. My shortcut in the gnome menu looks like this:

env WINEPREFIX="/home/risto/.wine-fo3" env=WINEDEBUG="fixme-all" /home/risto/.wine-fo3/wine-1.1.20/wine "C:\Program Files\Bethesda Softworks\Fallout 3\Fallout3.exe"

(Modify for paths of course, and create the wineprefix and activate it before installing Fallout). Very simple and much easier than trying to figure out why Fallout 3 and Lotro just really don't run with the same Wine settings =)

---

### Post by ajackson on 2009-05-21
> **worry said:**
> Ubuntu 9.04 I am using.
Currently using ALSA have tried OSS, no changes.
Patching do work and I have done so without any errors.

After removing the fixme-all line its output is this single line: "fixme:faultrep:ReportFault 0x32ec00 0x0 stub"
That's odd because you never seem to get a faultrep fixme without getting something else before it. But if you are certain that is all that is being produced then I'll take your word for it.

My advice, file a bug over at [http://winehq.org]("http://winehq.org").

### Post by ajackson on 2009-05-21
> **worry said:**
> After the login the "Launch Game - Wine Output" window pops up and gives me that line and then just stays like it.
It is exactly the same with a stable release of Wine just to make it clear.
Make up your mind it either does nothing or does something (I consider displaying another window as "something"). You are making it very hard for me to help you.

Lets try one last time.

Have you installed anything else in the same wine prefix?
Set the sound option to OSS and only OSS.
Post the output of wine --version
Set WINEDEBUG to blank.
Use the config checker and post what it says.
Try the patcher and post the output.
Then try to start the game, post any output.

> It seems weird I cant get the game running at all seeing everything else is working.
It seems weird that you are trying to make it impossible to support you.

Edit:
> What does a faultrep actually mean? What can they be?
faultrep is an internal function used when wine is reporting a fault, it isn't fully implemented and is usually the last call before wine gives up trying. That is why I was surprised to see a faultrep without any additional messages.

If you're installing vc2005 with winetricks before installing lotro, try not doing it.  Create a new prefix and just install the game.

I initially followed the instructions on appdb about installing vc2005 and all that and the game simply wouldn't work.  Letting the lotro installer install dotnet and the VC runtime stuff itself fixed the problem.  You'll probably still want to set your video memory in the registry though.  Using pylotro, I found most of the steps in the appdb guide unnecessary.

I'm running 9.04 myself with the latest wine, so I can also confirm it does work fine (with the exception of my weird mouse issue mentioned earlier in the thread).

Edit:
[QUOTE=worry]Just to be sure uninstall Wine, remove .wine folder reinstall it and copy my fresh lotro folder into the new one?[/QUOTE]
No need to uninstall wine, just remove the the .wine folder.  Running winecfg will recreate a fresh .wine folder.

### Post by ajackson on 2009-05-21
> **Devilman13 said:**
> Thank you for the description on prefixes. I'm still a noob with that and the last time I tried it I screwed things up pretty good lol. However, it's something I need to really get used to. I find certain parts of your instructions on using different Wine prefixes slightly confusing
Prefixes are one of those things that once you understand them you wonder what all the fuss was about. Think of them as virtual machines, each prefix is in essence a unique version of Windows, one might have Office in it and be WinXP while another can be Win2000 and contain LotRO.

> **I'm thinking about keeping my existing Wine install I built from the 1.1.21 source and trying to put lotro in it's own bottle with all of the D3D registry stuff removed.** Would this work out for me? Or am I thinking of this backwards?

### Post by BatPenguin on 2009-05-21
> **Devilman13 said:**
> I'm thinking about keeping my existing Wine install I built from the 1.1.21 source and trying to put lotro in it's own bottle with all of the D3D registry stuff removed. Would this work out for me? Or am I thinking of this backwards? IE; must LOTRO be running in basic wine 1.1.20 from the package manager and I somehow build 1.1.21 with the D3D patch seperately and it has it's own prefix or uhhh... 

Yes, I think that's backwards. I would keep the repository wine (WineHQ) version the one that you use for Lotro and everything else, and let it update itself whenever it wants to, and use the compiled wine for nothing but Fallout 3 and keep it seperate.

The wineprefix page you posted looks pretty hardcore to me, frankly...I think I just looked at this:

[http://wiki.jswindle.com/index.php/Wine_Prefixes](http://wiki.jswindle.com/index.php/Wine_Prefixes)

and did:

```

mkdir .wine-fo3
export WINEPREFIX=$HOME/.wine-fo3/
winecfg

```

Then I downloaded the wine sources to ~/.wine-fo3/wine, patched and compiled it, did the regedits and used the wine program I just compiled to install Fallout. See the launcher that I posted for how I make sure it's the compiled wine that starts the game later on every time. Note that if you close your terminal during this, you'll have to do the "export" command again. This method is probably not as elegant as those bottles described on your page, but it works for me and was pretty simple to do, really.

If others are struggling with the prefixes, I think Crossover games handles these bottles somehow nicely, probably worth checking out - not that I really know anything about it, heh, but that's what I've read. I should check it out sometime. I used to pay for Cedega but stopped that when they didn't seem to ever update their software. Crossover probably would be a nice and a simpler way to run games and other Windows programs and make sure their settings stay "clean", and I've actually many times meant to look into it but never seem to actually get around to it :)

If it requires .NET, then you need to do that step before attempting the installer as it will foul up your ~/.wine prefix.

so, open a terminal and do:

mv ~/.wine ~/.wine-old
wget kegel.com/wine/winetricks
sh winetricks dotnet20

Now run the setup again.
